posters.title: FIRMAMENT, QUANTUM WALKS,
posters.transcription:
FIRMAMENT,  
QUANTUM WALKS,  
AND CREATION

A speculative but formal analogy built from  
recent graph-theoretic quantum walk constructions

UPPER  
REGION  
(ORDERED  
POSSIBILITIES)

FIRMAMENT  
Graph boundary  
(lawful partition  
enabling relation)

BOUNDARY  
(DISTINCTION)

LOWER  
REGION  
(REALIZED  
ACTUALITIES)

EMERGENT  
PATTERNS  
(LAWFUL  
ACTUALIZATION)

1  
Starting point

Take the firmament not as a sky-object,  
but as a structure of distinction:  
a lawful partition that makes  
ordered relation possible.

2  
Formal ingredients

Graph G=(V,E); adjacency matrix A;  
continuous-time walk U(t)=e^{itH};  
perturbations, tails, chiral phases,  
and weak couplings.

3  
Tamon-inspired constructions

Spatial search, state transfer,  
infinite tails, weak-coupling  
effective dynamics, and  
chiral uniform mixing.

4  
Claim

If these constructions are read  
metaphysically, creation can be pictured  
not as static inventory, but as lawful  
actualization across a structured  
possibility-space.

EVOLUTION (CONTINUOUS-TIME WALK)

U(t) = e^{itH}

STATES / POSSIBLE TRANSITIONS

1  
2  
3  
4  
5  
6

edge (E)

possible  
transition

Important: analogy,  
not theorem.

The mathematics does  
not prove theology;  
it supplies a disciplined  
vocabulary for  
thinking it.

Series roadmap: 1. frame · 2. distinction · 3. lawful unfolding · 4. search ·  
5. weak coupling · 6. tails · 7. chirality · 8. synthesis

1 / 8
posters.visual: A dark celestial poster with gold serif typography frames a glowing dome-like graph boundary over a cliff edge, separating an upper blue network of possibilities from a lower gold network of realized actualities. The central diagram uses nodes, edges, dashed guide lines, and a vertical axis to make the “firmament” read as a mathematical partition rather than a sky object. Lower panels combine numbered argument boxes, a continuous-time quantum walk formula, a small state-transition graph, and an ornate warning box in a scholarly, cosmic, illuminated-manuscript style.

posters.title: FIRMAMENT = STRUCTURE OF DISTINCTION
posters.transcription:
FIRMAMENT =  
STRUCTURE OF DISTINCTION

Separation does not annihilate relation; it orders it.

UPPER  
REGION  
(ORDERED  
POSSIBILITIES)

firmament /  
lawful partition

LOWER  
REGION  
(REALIZED  
ACTUALITIES)

1  
Metaphysical reading

The firmament is not a thing among things.  
It is the condition under which above and  
below, inside and outside, can be  
meaningfully distinct.

2  
Graph-theoretic reading

Let G = (V,E). Vertices represent states;  
edges represent admissible transitions.  
A boundary, partition, or quotient  
organizes which relations are possible  
and how strongly.

Formal sketch

Graph = (V,E)  
node = state  
edge = possible transition  
boundary = firmament  
region = realm

3  
Why distinction matters

Creation begins not by filling an  
undifferentiated whole with objects,  
but by instituting separability,  
adjacency, and lawful passage.

Key claim

To create first is to differentiate;  
to differentiate is not to destroy  
relation, but to render relation  
intelligible.

Series roadmap: 1. frame · 2. distinction · 3. lawful unfolding · 4. search ·  
5. weak coupling · 6. tails · 7. chirality · 8. synthesis

2 / 8
posters.visual: A dark celestial poster uses gold serif typography and thin ornamental borders, with a starfield, crescent moon, and galaxy behind the title. The central diagram is a glowing dome-like graph split by a horizontal boundary: blue nodes and edges above, amber nodes and edges below, labeled as upper possibilities and lower actualities. Lower panels combine explanatory text, a smaller formal graph legend, a key claim box, and a roadmap footer, all in an illuminated manuscript or esoteric technical-chart style.

posters.title: CREATIO CONTINUA =
posters.transcription:
CREATIO CONTINUA =  
LAWFUL UNFOLDING

Reality as sustained evolution across a structured possibility-space

earlier

TIME

later

state \(t_1\)  
state \(t_2\)  
state \(t_3\)  
⋮  
state \(t_n\)

1  
Continuous-  
time walk

For a self-adjoint  
generator H, the  
evolution is  
\(U(t)=e^{itH}\).  
The graph is not  
merely drawn;  
it is dynamical.

\(U(t)=e^{itH}\)

\(U(t)\) = evolution  
\(H\) = generator  
\(t\) = time

2  
Theological  
analogue

Creatio continua  
means creation is not  
only an originating act.  
Reality persists because  
it is continuously  
sustained and  
unfolded.

3  
Interpretive bridge

The Hamiltonian is not God. But as a metaphor of lawful  
sustaining agency, it models a world whose becoming is  
structured rather than arbitrary.

past → present → future

one law, ongoing actualization

Precision

The claim is not that  
the world literally is a  
quantum walk, but that  
continuous-time  
evolution provides a  
disciplined image of  
ongoing creation.

Series roadmap: 1. frame • 2. distinction • 3. lawful unfolding • 4. search •  
5. weak coupling • 6. tails • 7. chirality • 8. synthesis

3 / 8
posters.visual: A dark, star-filled art-deco poster with gold serif typography frames a central layered network diagram, where blue-white graph states unfold downward through time while golden streams connect them to a bright source above. The composition uses labeled state slices, a time arrow, boxed explanatory panels, and a bottom timeline from past to present to future to connect mathematical evolution with theological interpretation. Gold ornamental borders, celestial imagery, and the luminous equation panel give the piece a formal, mystical-scientific visual language.

posters.title: SPATIAL SEARCH = GLOBAL TO LOCAL
posters.transcription:
SPATIAL SEARCH =
GLOBAL TO LOCAL

From principal eigenvector to marked vertex

GLOBAL STATE  
φ

PERTURBATION  
|w><w|

LOCALIZED STATE  
e_w

Amplitude initially  
spread globally

w

Amplitude concentrates  
at the marked vertex w

H = γA + |w><w|

1 Construction  
Start with adjacency matrix A  
and principal eigenvector φ.  
Mark a target vertex w by  
adding a rank-one perturbation.

2 Search Hamiltonian  
H = γA + |w><w|

3 What happens  
The global state φ is rotated  
toward the localized state e_w.  
Search is a passage from  
distributed amplitude to  
local manifestation.

4 Spectral ingredient  
Performance depends on the  
overlap |<e_w,φ>| — the target’s  
spectral 'shadow' inside the  
principal state.

GLOBAL STATE /  
PRINCIPAL EIGENVECTOR

Amplitude is spread  
across the whole  
structure.

MARKED TARGET VERTEX

The perturbation  
privileges one  
local site.

Caveat  
This does not mean God searches from ignorance.  
The theological analogy reads search as actualization,  
not lack of knowledge.

4 / 8
posters.visual: A dark starfield poster with ornate gold borders and classical serif typography, arranged as an explanatory infographic. The top half contrasts two network diagrams: a blue globally illuminated graph labeled φ on the left, transformed by a gold perturbation arrow into a graph with one bright localized marked vertex w on the right. The lower half uses framed numbered panels to explain construction, Hamiltonian, dynamics, and spectral overlap, with small graph thumbnails, a scales icon for the caveat, and a gold page marker.

posters.title: WEAK COUPLING = SMALL CAUSES,
posters.transcription:
WEAK COUPLING =  
SMALL CAUSES,  
LARGE EFFECTS

Tiny pendant edges can reorganize reachability

probe

ε

ε

target

①  
Construction

Take a base graph and  
attach pendant vertices  
by weak edges of  
strength ε. The tiny  
attachments are the  
‘arms’; the large  
graph is the body.

②  
Mechanism

Although ε is small,  
perturbation theory  
produces an effective  
low-dimensional  
dynamics on the arm  
subspace.

③  
Interpretive point

Weak does not mean  
irrelevant. A minute  
structural relation can  
redirect the whole  
walk.

coupling strength = ε

effective Hamiltonian on a reduced subspace

H_eff = [ E_p   εκ  
          εκ^*  E_t ]

T.rex sketch

big graph with two tiny arms

probe

target

ε

ε

Metaphysical temptation

Small causes, faint relations, or slight  
perturbations may have outsized  
consequences because the whole  
structure mediates them.

5. weak coupling • 6. tails • 7. chirality • 8. synthesis
posters.visual: A tall, dark celestial poster uses a gold serif title above a glowing blue network diagram, with tiny dashed “probe” and “target” arms attached by ε-strength edges. Below, ornate bordered panels explain construction, mechanism, interpretation, an effective Hamiltonian, a small “T.rex sketch,” and a metaphysical note. The style is cosmic and academic: navy starfield background, moon and galaxy imagery, gold ornamentation, white body text, and luminous graph nodes emphasizing how small couplings can reshape global reachability.

posters.title: FINITE GRAPH, INFINITE TAILS
posters.transcription:
FINITE GRAPH,  
INFINITE TAILS

Protected transfer inside a larger Hilbert space

A  
B

1  
Construction

Attach one or more infinite paths to a finite graph. The total system becomes infinite-dimensional, but structured local behavior can survive.

2  
Mechanism

Invariant-subspace / Jacobi-matrix reductions reveal protected or dark subspaces in which transfer persists despite the tails.

3  
Interpretive point

Infinity need not erase every local form. Local order can remain intelligible even when embedded in an unbounded outside.

\(|\psi_A> \rightarrow |\psi_B>\)  
\(H_{local} \subset H_{total}\)

inside the finite graph

A  
B

signal transfer succeeds because a protected corridor minimizes leakage

into the abyss (outside)

Use with care

The picture is not “soul escaping matter”; it is a formal image of locality preserved inside a larger and partially leaking world.

6 / 8
posters.visual: A dark, starfield poster uses gold serif typography and thin ornamental borders, with luminous blue network lines over a domed city-like finite graph. The central diagram shows nodes and edges connecting labeled points A and B, while many glowing tails descend outward into vortex-like abyss structures below. Three numbered explanatory panels sit beneath the main diagram, followed by a boxed Hilbert-space formula and a bottom comparison strip contrasting the finite graph with leakage into the outside.

posters.title: CHIRALITY = ORIENTATION OF POSSIBILITY
posters.transcription:
CHIRALITY =  
ORIENTATION OF POSSIBILITY

Handedness changes interference,  
and interference changes what is reachable

ORDINARY ADJACENCY

1  
2  
5  
4  
5

Muted interference, limited transport  
and mixing

CHIRAL ADJACENCY

1  
2  
3  
4  
5

e^{i\phi_5}  
e^{i\phi_1}  
e^{i\phi_2}  
e^{i\phi_3}  
e^{i\phi_4}

Rich interference, enhanced transport  
and mixing

1 Ordinary case  
Real symmetric adjacency constrains  
the dynamics. Many transport and  
mixing phenomena obey no-go  
theorems in this setting.

2 Chiral construction  
Replace ordinary edge weights by  
a unitary signing \sigma. The walk uses  
a Hermitian matrix A^\sigma whose edges  
carry phases.

3 Formal condition  
\sigma_{uv} is a unit complex number and  
\sigma_{vu} = \overline{\sigma_{uv}}. Orientation changes  
interference without sacrificing  
Hermiticity.

4 Interpretive point  
The same underlying graph can  
acquire new capacities once  
possibility is oriented rather than  
merely connected.

phase on edges

e^{i\phi}

unitary signing

Consequences  
Chirality can enable state transfer or uniform mixing  
that are forbidden, or much harder, in the ordinary case.

Series roadmap: 1. frame · 2. distinction · 3. lawful unfolding · 4. search ·  
5. weak coupling · 6. tails · 7. chirality · 8. synthesis

7 / 8
posters.visual: A dark, starry, gold-trimmed poster compares ordinary graph adjacency on the left with directed chiral adjacency on the right, divided by a vertical gold line. The left diagram shows an undirected graph and muted blue spectrum, while the right shows a directed cycle with phase labels and a bright rainbow interference spectrum. Ornamental borders, celestial imagery, serif typography, and symbols like \(e^{i\phi}\) frame chirality as both a mathematical and metaphysical orientation principle.

posters.title: SYNTHESIS: GOD CREATES BY
posters.transcription:
SYNTHESIS:  
GOD CREATES BY  
MAKING GOODNESS REACHABLE?

A metaphysical reading of the formal constructions

1

Firmament →  
structure of  
distinction

Creation first  
institutes lawful  
separability: the  
world becomes  
articulate before  
it becomes  
populated.

2

Continuous-time  
walk →  
creatio continua

Reality is pictured  
as sustained lawful  
unfolding rather  
than as a single  
inert outcome.

3

Search →  
actualization  
without ignorance

Theological ‘search’  
is not epistemic lack;  
it is a disciplined  
metaphor for  
drawing a local good  
out of a structured  
whole.

4

Weak coupling →  
disproportionate  
causality

Slight relations can  
redirect entire paths  
because the whole  
network mediates  
them.

5

Chirality →  
orientation of  
possibility

Not only matter and  
law, but orientation  
and phase, affect  
what the world  
can do.

6

Goodness →  
what becomes  
reachable

A good is not  
merely named;  
it becomes  
dynamically  
accessible under the  
right ordering of  
the whole.

GOODNESS

God creates by making Goodness reachable.

Analogy, not proof.

Strongest version

The point is not that graph theory proves theology.  
It is that these constructions offer a precise imaginative vocabulary  
for speaking about creation, order, and the manifestation of the good.

8 / 8
posters.visual: A dark celestial poster with gold serif typography frames six numbered text panels around a central glowing geometric dome and vertical beam labeled “GOODNESS.” Network diagrams, orbital arrows, graph nodes, and path structures surround the center, visually linking graph theory and metaphysical ordering. The style is ornate and esoteric, using star fields, gold borders, blue-white light, and manuscript-like panel decorations.

The central diagram shows a rough vertical boundary dividing a blank unmarked left field from a speckled marked right field, with pointer lines labeling “UNMARKED STATE,” “MARKED STATE,” and the red “BOUNDARY.” Typography mixes very large high-contrast serif title lettering with tightly tracked uppercase sans-serif labels and a small italic quotation section near the bottom.

posters.visual: A tall illuminated-manuscript style poster uses a dark navy and antique gold palette with ornate borders, decorated initials, and serif typography. The central diagram shows a cosmic horizon line splitting an upper celestial region of arcs, stars, and ordered geometry from a lower region of flowing terrain-like contours and realized structures. Side annotations use circular icons and arrows to frame the firmament as a lawful partition, while the bottom parchment inset presents the thesis as a formal caption.

posters.title: LAW OF FORMS: MARK, STATE, CROSSING
posters.transcription:
LAW OF FORMS:  
MARK, STATE, CROSSING

THE SPENCER–BROWN  
MARK  
[ ] ( · )  
TO DRAW A DISTINCTION  
IS TO MAKE A FORM.

1 DISTINCTION

U

I

A mark divides  
the universe (U)  
into two states:  
inside (I)  
and outside (U).

The form arises from  
the act of distinction.

2 INDICATION

U

I

An indication  
selects a state.  
It does not name  
an object; it  
indicates a side  
of the distinction.

3 CROSSING

U

I

Crossing is the  
operation that  
moves from one  
state to the other.  
It requires a mark—  
a boundary—to  
be crossed.

4 RE-ENTRY

U

I

Re-entry occurs  
when a crossing  
returns to the  
state it came from,  
but from within.  
It enables recursion  
and self-reference.

A mark is not an object;  
it is an operation  
that produces a state  
and the possibility of  
crossing between states.

Distinction is  
primitive;  
objects are  
derivative.

MARKED FIELD VS UNMARKED FIELD

U

states.

only a single, undivided state (U)

SERIES: DISTINCTION AS PRIMITIVE • 3 / 8
posters.visual: The poster uses a tall cream-paper layout with bold condensed black title typography, accented blue for “STATE” and red for “CROSSING.” Diagrams show a rough black square mark separating inside `I` from outside `U`, with arrows illustrating indication, crossing, and re-entry. A small architectural head illustration and compass-like symbol reinforce the philosophical theme, while the bottom strip contrasts marked and unmarked fields with a red crossing arrow.

posters.title: SYNTHESIS: GOD CREATES
posters.transcription:
SYNTHESIS:

GOD CREATES  
BY  
DRAWING DISTINCTIONS

1. MARK

The first act:  
a mark is placed  
within the undifferentiated.  
This is not a thing,  
but a distinction.

THE UNDIFFERENTIATED  
The Plenum / Pure Potential

THE FIRMAMENT  
The First Distinction  
A boundary is drawn.

4. WALK

A walk is a sequence  
that respects adjacency.

Choice actualizes  
a path.

2. FIRMAMENT

The mark draws a boundary.  
Inside and outside  
are distinguished.  
A region becomes possible.

THE GRAPH OF POSSIBLE TRAVERSAL  
Adjacency without Necessity.  
Relations become possible.

5. SEARCH

Intelligence explores  
the graph.  
Search seeks  
the better, the true,  
the good.

3. GRAPH

Within the region,  
adjacency relations  
are defined.  
A graph of possible  
traversal arises.

WALKS BECOME REAL  
Paths actualized through choice,  
order, and seeking.

GOODNESS

6. GOODNESS  
REACHABLE

Through walks and search,  
the good is discovered  
and made reachable.  
Manifestation occurs.

THE CHAIN OF CREATION BY DISTINCTION

MARK → BOUNDARY → REGION → ADJACENCY → WALK → SEARCH → MANIFESTATION

THEOLOGICAL PRECISION: Analogy, not proof.  
This diagram speaks truly of God only as analogy permits, not as demonstration.

God creates not merely by making things,  
but by instituting the distinctions under which  
the good can appear, relate, and become reachable.

SERIES: DISTINCTION AS PRIMITIVE • 8 / 8
posters.visual: A dark navy and gold illuminated-manuscript style poster is organized around a central vertical cosmological diagram, flanked by six numbered explanatory panels. The center imagery descends from a galaxy-like undifferentiated plenum to a glowing boundary disk, graph network, winding walk, and radiant “GOODNESS” node. Gold line art, serif typography, thin ornamental borders, stars, graph nodes, arrows, a magnifying glass, and radiant bursts visually encode distinction, adjacency, search, and manifestation.

posters.title: WORLDSYSTEMS AS ORDERED CREATION · 7/8

posters.title: WORLDSYSTEMS AS ORDERED CREATION · 5/8
posters.transcription:
WORLDSYSTEMS AS ORDERED CREATION · 5/8 FINITE ORDER, OPEN HORIZON Embedding a finite worldsystem in an infinite exterior open exterior / horizon / environment boundary channel localized mode protected subspace finite ordered interior boundary channel open exterior / horizon / environment finite city in an infinite abyss CONCEPTS protected subspace maintain local coherence localized mode confined excitations boundary channel coupling to exterior open exterior arbitrarily extended horizon no returned signal ENGINEERING USE control inflow / outflow attach observers or reservoirs preserve local sectors expose the world to infinity without dissolving it HOW FINITE ORDER PERSISTS • Local rules define a closed algebra on the interior. • Boundary channels couple only a controlled interface. • Information, energy, or influence may flow outward. • As long as protected subspaces remain isolated from return paths, interior order is preserved. • Local dissipation at the boundary prevents back-reflection of disorder. DECOMPOSITION OF THE WORLD H = H_core + H_tails + H_coupling finite interior infinite exterior boundary interaction H_core : Hamiltonian of the finite ordered interior H_tails : Hamiltonian of the exterior tails / environment H_coupling : Coupling across boundary channels Engineering objective: Choose channels and couplings so that desired sectors remain stable while beneficial exchange occurs. CORE VS. TAILS: FLOW MAP protected (retained) remains in interior leaking (exported) flows into tails boundary channel EXAMPLE: SEMI-INFINITE TAILS ... ... Each tail extends half-infinite. No signal returns from infinity. The core remains finite and ordered. CHANNEL CHARACTERISTICS capacity limits rate of exchange selectivity filters what can pass dissipation absorbs and damps directionality prefer outward flow decoupling reduce coupling to preserve order STABILITY CRITERION (SKETCH) Let P project onto a protected subspace of the interior. d/dt

≈ -Γ_leak + Γ_stabilize < 0 If leakage rate Γ_leak is kept below the stabilizing rate Γ_stabilize, the subspace remains effectively protected. Design channels so that Γ_leak is small for protected sectors. An engineered creation can be finite in structure yet open in horizon: bounded order, unbounded exterior. mark order adjacency evolution search manifestation

The imagery blends cosmology, systems theory, and sacred-geometry motifs, including spirals, lattices, boundary rings, and icon sequences.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 1 / 8
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 1 / 8

FROM GOODNESS  
TO ACTION

Recasting Tamon-style worldsystems  
as action-minimizing architectures.

“We shift the  
objective from  
seeking the greatest  
good to enabling the  
least action.

Old objective

maximize good  
Search the state space to reach  
a privileged good region.

x_0  
W  
w*

the one with least action S[p].

S[p] action of path p

ACTION FORMALISM

S[p] = \sum_t L(x_t, x_{t+1})

p* = \argmin_p S[p]

Search becomes path  
selection under cost.

ORDERED STATE SPACE (POSÉT) AND COVER GRAPH

THE LEAST-ACTION ARCHITECTURE CHAIN

distinction  
differences arise;  
entities are

order

arrangements;

INTERPRETATION

The worldsystem need not be engineered to  
chase the greatest good directly. It may instead  
be engineered so that admissible trajectories  
settle onto paths of least action, and only  
indirectly yield stable goods.

ENGINEER  
THE LANDSCAPE  
(order, adjacency,  
cost structure)

EMERGES ENDOGENOUSLY

QUESTIONS

1 What counts as action?

2 Is action local or global?

3 Do low-action paths always

4 Can minimization recurse  
across scales?

LINKS TO EARLIER INGREDIENTS

spatial search

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 2 / 8
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 2 / 8

ACTION ON AN  
ORDERED WORLD

Defining a cost functional on a poset and its cover graph.

“Action prices  
lawful change.”  
— least-action  
principle

ORDERED STATE SPACE (POSET) AND COVER GRAPH

higher  
(order)

FINITE POSET (PARTIAL ORDER)

COVER GRAPH (HASSE DIAGRAM)

x_T  
top

Rank 4  
Rank 3  
Rank 2  
Rank 1  
Rank 0

x_0  
bottom

How to read this  
• Every upward edge respects the order (x ≤ y).  
• Ranks increase by 1 along each cover.  
• Moves are only allowed along cover edges.

state  
state (in cover graph)  
≤ (order relation)  
cover (immediate relation)

WHAT CONTRIBUTES  
TO ACTION?

GEOMETRY (DISTANCE)  
Spatial separation between  
adjacent states.

ASCENT THROUGH ORDER  
Climbing to higher rank  
carries a price.

SWITCHING COST  
Changing direction or choice  
incurs a penalty.

BOUNDARY CROSSINGS  
Leaving the interior or  
entering forbidden/rare  
regions costs more.

WEAK LINKS  
Edges with low capacity  
or reliability add cost.

DISSIPATION / DRAG  
Irreversible effort, friction,  
or information loss.

COST CONTRIBUTIONS  
geometry  
other penalties  
order ascent  
sum (total action)

LOCAL ACTION  
DENSITY

DISCRETE LAGRANGIAN (LOCAL COST)

L(x_t, x_{t+1}) = α · d(x_t, x_{t+1})  
+ β · Δ rank(x_t, x_{t+1})  
+ χ · 1_∂(x_t, x_{t+1})  
+ η · w(x_t, x_{t+1})  
+ ζ · diss(x_t, x_{t+1})

d        distance (geometry)  
Δ rank   rank change (0 or +1)  
1_∂      boundary crossing (0 or 1)  
w        weak-link penalty (≥ 0)  
diss     dissipation / drag (≥ 0)

PATH ACTION

S[p] = Σ_t L(x_t, x_{t+1})

Total action is the sum of  
local costs along the path.

EXAMPLE PATHS  
FROM x_0 TO x_T

LOW-ACTION PATH (Preferred)

x_T  
x_0

Step costs    Value  
geometry      6.0  
order ascent  3.0  
switching     1.0

TOTAL ACTION 11.0

MEDIUM-ACTION PATH

x_T  
x_0

Step costs    Value  
geometry      9.0  
order ascent  4.0

All paths start at x_0 (bottom) and end at x_T (top). Only cover edges are used.

DESIGN CHOICE

We do not merely declare a goal  
and hope to reach it.

We engineer the worldsystem  
by engineering:

ADJACENCY  
who can connect to whom

LOCAL COSTS  
what each move costs

CONSTRAINTS  
which moves are legal

FEEDBACK  
how costs are perceived  
and learned

Right structure, right prices,  
right flows.

THESIS

..

Once the state space is ordered, action is a rule that assigns price  
to every lawful move. Trajectories differ not only by destination,  
but by what they cost to realize.

THE PROCESS

DISTINCTION  
Differentiate the  
state space.

ORDER  
Impose a partial  
order (poset).

LOCAL COST  
Assign prices to  
lawful moves.

ACCUMULATED ACTION  
S[p]  
Sum local costs  
along a path.

LOW-ACTION ROUTE  
Select the path  
of least action.

SERIES ROADMAP (8 PARTS)  
1 Order & Intention • 2 Order & Prices • 3 Action & Path Selection • 4 Path Ensembles • 5 Open Systems • 6 Emergent Goods • 7 Scaling & Recursion • 8 Realization

posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 3 / 8

LOW-ACTION  
SEARCH

From quantum-walk search  
to stationary-action path selection.

“We prefer  
the paths that  
nature herself  
is most willing  
to take.”

TWO VIEWS OF DYNAMICS

Ordinary marked search

Find the marked target w  
by exploring the graph.

x_0  
w

• diffuse exploration  
• no preference among admissible paths  
• arrival amplitude spreads across many routes

VS.

Low-action search

Many admissible paths compete;  
low-action trajectories dominate.

p_1  
p_2  
p*  
p_3  
x_0  
x_T = w

low action (high weight)  
medium action  
high action (low weight)

PATH ENSEMBLE

A(x_0 -> x_T) = \sum_{p \in P(x_0,x_T)} e^{-S[p]/\tau}

S[p] = \sum_t L(x_t, x_{t+1})     (action of path p)  
\tau = action temperature (control sharpness)

Lower action S[p]  
receives higher  
effective weight.

QUANTUM IDIOM (COMPARE)

In a more quantum setting,  
compare phase weights

A_q(x_0 -> x_T) = \sum_p e^{iS[p]/\hbar}

Interference favors  
stationary-action paths.

CANDIDATE PATHS THROUGH AN ORDERED GRAPH

Path action  
(relative)

low  
medium

indicates  
effective weight  
(e^{-S/\tau}).

x_T = w

(lower weight)

(higher weight)

(or amplitude

Low-action search yields earlier,  
sharper arrival.

INTERPRETATION

“Search is no longer merely  
‘find the good node’;  
it becomes ‘distribute amplitude  
over lawful trajectories and let  
low-action routes dominate.’”

CONSEQUENCES

CONCENTRATION  
Amplitude concentrates  
on efficient paths,  
accelerating arrival.

ROBUSTNESS  
Graceful adaptation  
to perturbations  
in the landscape.

AVOIDANCE OF  
BRITTLE DETOURS  
High-action cul-de-sacs  
naturally carry little  
weight.

PATH COMPETITION  
Multiple good routes  
can share the load  
constructively.

THESIS

Action minimization replaces explicit maximization of goodness  
with a lawful bias toward efficient trajectories.

ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 4 / 8

WEAK COUPLING,  
LOW-ACTION STEERING.

Small interventions can open  
disproportionately cheap trajectories.

“Small design  
can shift the  
cost landscape  
more than large  
force can.”

BEFORE: core order, costly options

p_1  
x_T  
goal  
p_2  
p_3

ε

structure

attached channel or corridor

FORMAL VIEW

H = H_0 + εV

H_0  unperturbed (core) dynamics  
εV  weak coupling (|ε| << 1)

Action reinterpretation:  
A tiny coupling can reduce total path action  
by opening a shortcut or selective corridor.

PATH ACTIONS BEFORE AND AFTER WEAK LINK

Path | Before (ε = 0) |  | After (ε ≠ 0) |  | Change  
 | Action S[p] | Rank | Action S_ε[p] | Rank | ΔS

p* (new via link) | — | — | 12.3 | 1 | —  
p_1 | 24.7 | 1 | 18.9 | 2 | −5.8  
p_2 | 31.2 | 2 | 27.1 | 3 | −4.1  
p_3 | 36.5 | 3 | 34.8 | 4 | −1.7  
… | … | … | … | … | …

Weak coupling introduces a new minimum (p*) and lowers other costs slightly.

PATH ACTION vs ROUTE INDEX

higher  
(costlier)

Before (ε = 0)  
After (ε ≠ 0)

Action  
S[p]

lower  
(cheaper)

p*

new minimum  
created by weak  
channel

Route index (ranked by action)

ENGINEERING USES

Create narrow passages  
insert sparse links or corridors

Redirect amplitude  
guide flows toward  
cheaper trajectories

Lower switching cost locally  
change economics without  
forcing everywhere

Preserve global order  
maintain the core structure  
while changing route economics

INTERPRETATION

Before: all corridors costly

After: a weak channel reshapes  
the landscape—certain lawful  
trajectories become much cheaper.

Weak coupling does not  
force the system.  
It reshapes the cost  
landscape so that  
some trajectories  
become available  
at far lower action.

KEY RELATIONSHIPS

|ε| small ⇒ structural stability

ΔS ~ O(ε) ⇒ first-order cost shifts

New path appears when: S_ε[p*] < min_i S_ε[p_i]

Design goal: minimize S_ε[p*] with minimal |ε|

THESIS (PARAPHRASE)

Control by least action can be subtle:  
not coercion, but selective cheapening of lawful transitions.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 5 / 8
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 5 / 8

OPEN SYSTEMS,  
EFFECTIVE ACTION

How tails, reservoirs, and dissipation  
reshape least-action behavior.

“The world is  
not closed.  
Boundaries  
are choices.”

SYSTEM SCHEMATIC

RESERVOIRS / TAILS  
(open channels)

absorber /  
dissipator A

absorber /  
dissipator B

ORDERED CORE  
(finite region of higher order)

RESERVOIRS / TAILS  
(open channels)

absorber /  
dissipator C

absorber /  
dissipator D

trajectories that remain within the core  
trajectories that exit into reservoirs  
dissipative/outgoing flow (not returned)

EFFECTIVE ACTION

S_eff[p] = S_core[p] + S_boundary[p] + S_diss[p]

S_core[p]  
action accumulated within the ordered core

S_boundary[p]  
cost/benefit of crossing or using boundaries

S_diss[p]  
dissipation from export of disorder or energy

Open boundaries can make some trajectories effectively  
cheaper or more stable by dissipating unwanted return flow.

WHY OPENNESS MATTERS

1 EXPORT OF DISORDER  
Offloads entropy, noise, and errors to reservoirs.

2 PREVENTION OF BACK-REFLECTION  
Reduces returns, echoes, and cyclic waste.

3 STABILIZATION OF SELECTED CHANNELS  
Favors useful flows and useful information.

4 MAINTENANCE OF LOCAL ORDER  
Keeps the core coherent, responsive, and adaptive.

TRAJECTORY LANDSCAPE

CLOSED SYSTEM (no tails)

returns build up cost

back-reflection  
traps trajectories

Confinement raises effective action.  
Trajectories face more returns and less escape.

OPEN SYSTEM (with tails/reservoirs)

dissipation lowers  
return cost

escape channels  
reduce effective  
cost

Outflow lowers effective action.  
Trajectories can leave, dissipate, or be filtered.

LINK TO SERIES: INFINITE TAILS & PROTECTED SUBSPACES

Infinite tails (earlier posters)  
provide the space and  
capacity for dissipation.

Protected subspaces in  
the core select which  
patterns are worth  
keeping.

INTERPRETATION

Least action in an open world is not  
purely internal; the environment enters  
the bookkeeping.

internal order + boundary exchange + dissipation = effective  
action

What leaves, what stays, and how it is routed—  
all affect the total.

THESIS  
PARAPHRASE

A worldsystem may minimize action not in isolation,  
but by trading with an exterior that absorbs, stabilizes, or filters trajectories.

SERIES ROADMAP (8 PARTS)  1 Birth of Order • 2 Order & Poise • 3 Action Design • 4 Path Ensembles • 5 Open Systems • 6 Emergent Goods • 7 Scaling & Recursion • 8 Realization

The central schematic shows an ordered core connected to reservoir/tail regions, with black, red, and dashed trajectories illustrating retained paths, exiting paths, and dissipative flow. Lower panels compare closed versus open action landscapes using 3D contour-like terrain diagrams, then connect the idea to earlier “infinite tails” and “protected subspaces” concepts through small symbolic diagrams.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 6 / 8
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 6 / 8

RECURSIVE  
LEAST ACTION

Can minimization instantiate itself across scales?

“The world  
minimizes by  
building systems  
that minimize  
within.”

PARADIGM SHIFT

1. NESTED ORDERED WORLD

LEGEND  
macro link  
macro node  
(subsystem)  
micro state  
optimal  
trajectory  
possible  
trajectory

Macro (world level):  
ordered graph  
of subsystems.

Each node contains  
an ordered system  
with its own  
least action.

ZOOM: SUBSYSTEM i

ZOOM: MICRO PATH IN i

x_0^i  
x_T^i  
optimal p_i^*

MACRO ACTION (OVER TRAJECTORY OF SUBSYSTEMS)

S_macro[P] = \sum_k L_macro(X_k, X_{k+1})

X_k = macro state of the world at step k  
(e.g., subsystem boundary states, summaries, flows)

P = {X_0, X_1, ..., X_K} = macro-trajectory

Minimize S_macro[P] over admissible macro-trajectories P.

MICRO ACTION WITHIN SUBSYSTEM i

S_i[p_i] = \sum_t L_i(x_t, x_{t+1})

x_t = micro state inside subsystem i

p_i = {x_0^i, x_1^i, ..., x_T^i} = micro-trajectory in i

Boundary / interface constraints couple i to the macro path.

Minimize S_i[p_i] over admissible micro-trajectories p_i.

REQUIREMENTS  
FOR RECURSION

1  
ADDITIVITY /  
COMPOSABILITY  
Total action decomposes  
into sum (or integral) of  
macro and local actions.

2  
STABLE INTERFACES  
Well-defined boundaries  
and exchanged  
quantities persist  
across variations.

3  
COARSE-GRAINING  
Macro variables capture  
the relevant impact of  
subsystems without  
needing full micro detail.

4  
SCALE SEPARATION  
Internal equilibration is  
sufficiently fast compared  
to macro evolution.

5  
COMPATIBLE  
LOCAL OBJECTIVES  
Local minima align with,  
and do not sabotage,  
global minimization.

2. COMPOSITION (RECURSION) CRITERION

Recursive instantiation works when  
local actions compose, scales separate,  
and subsystem minima do not  
destroy global minima.

Let P^* be a macro minimizer and p_i^* the micro  
minimizer inside each subsystem i.  
If boundary conditions induced by P^* are satisfied,  
and compatibility holds, then:

S_macro[P^*] + \sum_i S_i[p_i^*] = S_total^min

and (P^*, {p_i^*}) is a global minimizer.

WHAT RECURSION  
MEANS HERE

Not merely repeating  
the same picture—  
embedding action-  
minimizing subsystems  
inside an action-  
minimizing world.

NOT THIS  
(repetition)

THIS  
(embedding)

3. MICRO → MESO → MACRO CHAIN OF ACTION

MICRO (SUBSYSTEM LEVEL)

...

Action functional  
S_i[p_i] = \sum_t L_i(x_t, x_{t+1})

Action landscape  
S_i  
t  
local states

Minimize S_i over p_i

MESO (REGION / DOMAIN LEVEL)

...

Action functional  
S_meso[Q] = \sum_m L_meso(Y_m, Y_{m+1})

Action landscape  
S_meso  
regional configs

Minimize S_meso over Q

MACRO (WORLD LEVEL)

Action functional  
S_macro[P] = \sum_k L_macro(X_k, X_{k+1})

Action landscape  
S_macro  
world states

Minimize S_macro over P

VISUAL RECURSION SCHEMA

MACRO  
World  
S_macro

MESO  
Regions  
S_meso

MICRO  
Subsystems  
S_i

Information & constraints flow  
up and down; action flows across.

THESIS

Least action can recurse if the world decomposes into  
semi-autonomous layers whose local economies of action  
remain compatible with global order.

SERIES ROADMAP (8 PARTS)  
1 Shift of Objective  
2 Order & Focus  
3 Action Design  
4 Path Ensembles  
5 Open Systems  
6 Recursive Action  
7 Scaling & Recursion  
8 Realization

The palette is limited to black, red, teal, and blue, with vintage print texture, thick rules, mathematical notation, arrows, checkmarks, layered pyramids, and action-landscape mini-plots reinforcing the recursion-across-scales theme.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 7 / 8
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 7 / 8

WHERE RECURSION  
BREAKS

Why least-action minimization  
is not automatically self-similar.

“  
Local thrift  
can build  
expensive  
worlds.  
”

NESTED SCALES

LOCAL MINIMA CONFLICT WITH GLOBAL MINIMA

Worldsystem  
(macro)

Subsystems  
(meso)

Components  
(micro)

Trapped in  
a high-action  
macro basin.

Each subsystem  
minimizes its  
own action.

Parts do the  
same locally.

LOCAL OPTIMA

SCALE CONFLICT

GLOBAL TRAP

DIAGNOSTIC QUESTION

Does minimizing  
action locally  
preserve the  
global action  
economy,

or does it  
externalize cost  
to other scales?

IMPLICATION

Goods can fail to appear  
if the cheapest local  
motions create  
costly global structure.

Local economy  
≠  
Global economy

FAILURE MODES: WHY RECURSION BREAKS

1  
LOCAL MINIMA  
VS GLOBAL  
MINIMUM

Many small dips  
mislead search  
away from the  
global minimum.

2  
INCOMPATIBLE  
BOUNDARY  
CONDITIONS

Locally optimal states  
cannot be tiled  
without creating  
boundary strain.

3  
NON-ADDITIVE  
COUPLING

Interactions make the  
whole more (or less)  
than the sum of  
its parts.

4  
FRUSTRATION /  
CHIRALITY  
LOOPS

Loops force  
contradictory  
preferences;  
no state satisfies  
all locally.

5  
PATH DEPENDENCE  
& HYSTERESIS

History matters.  
Return path ≠  
forward path.  
Costs get locked in.

6  
SELF-REFERENCE  
COLLAPSE

Action of the system  
alters the rules  
it uses to evaluate  
action.

NON-COMPOSABILITY

ACTION DOES NOT COMPOSE UNDER STRONG INTERACTIONS.

S_total ≠ Σ_i S_i

In general:

S_total = Σ_i S_i + Σ_{i<j} I_ij + C_boundary + C_history + …

S_i  
local action of subsystem i

I_ij  
interaction term between i and j

C_boundary  
boundary / constraint cost

C_history  
path-dependence / hysteresis cost

PAIR INTERACTION EXAMPLE

1  
2  
3  
4

I_ij > 0  
synergy cost

I_ij < 0  
cooperative  
benefit

WHEN RECURSION HOLDS (CONDITIONAL)

Weak interactions  
Clean boundaries  
No hidden costs  
No frustrated loops  
No path dependence

I_ij ≈ 0, C_* ≈ 0

THEN S_total ≈ Σ_i S_i

CONSEQUENCE

LOCAL THRIFT CAN BUILD GLOBAL EXPENSIVENESS.

Parts: cheap motions

Locally cheap

=

Globally costly

Externalized costs appear as:

• Constraint buildup  
  (geometry, tension, congestion)  
• Fragile states (high sensitivity)  
• Latent costs (paid later)  
• Lost options (reduced flexibility)

What looks  
optimal here  
can be  
suboptimal  
elsewhere.

THESIS

RECURSIVE LEAST ACTION IS CONDITIONAL,  
NOT AUTOMATIC.

Strong interactions, hidden costs,  
and scale conflict can destroy the recursion.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 8/8
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 8/8

AN ACTION-MINIMIZING  
ORDERED CREATION

Final synthesis: when recursive least action does and does not work.

“ A world's  
structure is the  
shadow cast by  
its cheapest ways  
of becoming.

↑  
SYNTHESIS ARCHITECTURE  
↓

1  
distinction  
Differences  
arise;  
entities are  
distinguished.

2  
poset  
(order)  
Relations constrain  
possibilities;  
partial order  
emerges.

3  
cover graph  
Cover relations  
identify what can  
change locally.

4  
local action  
rule  
{ L(x_i,x_j) }  
Costs are assigned  
to local moves  
(least action rule).

5  
path  
ensemble  
x_0  
x_T  
Many trajectories  
compete;  
costs accumulate.

6  
low-action  
realization  
x_0  
x_T  
Least action paths  
(or families)  
dominate.

7  
emergent  
goods  
Stable, intelligible,  
often valuable  
structures appear.

RECURSION WORKS WHEN...

composable local actions  
Local costs can be composed without hidden terms.

stable interfaces  
Boundaries transmit predictable, low-cost summaries.

scale separation  
Relevant scales weakly interact; coarse-graining holds.

protected subspaces  
Robust sets persist; small perturbations stay contained.

compatible objectives  
Local objectives align with higher-level aims.

filtered openness  
Novelty enters through low-cost, informative channels.

RECURSION FAILS WHEN...

hidden interaction costs  
Unmodeled couplings make composition non-additive.

local/global conflict  
Locally optimal moves conflict at larger scales.

excessive feedback  
Tight loops amplify small errors and costs.

frustrated orientation  
Incompatible constraints prevent coherent ordering.

unstable boundaries  
Interfaces drift; coherence cannot be maintained.

EQUATIONS SUMMARY

S[p] = \sum_t L(x_t, x_{t+1})  
(path action)

p* = argmin_p S[p]  
(least-action path)

S_total ≈ \sum_i S_i

under weak interaction /  
good coarse-graining  
(composability condition)

RECURSIVE WORLDSYSTEMS

SUCCESS: recursive least action

Local economies compose cleanly.  
Interfaces are stable, costs add,  
structure recurs across scales.

FAILURE: recursion breaks

Hidden couplings and conflicts  
break composability.  
Action escalates with scale.

COMPOSABILITY CONDITION

For a partition of the system into  
subsystems i with interfaces ∂i,

S_total = \sum_i S_i + S_int

Recursion works when

S_int ≪ \sum_i S_i

(interfaces are cheap,  
information loss is small).

METAPHYSICAL READING

If reality were organized by lawful minimization of action,  
structured goods might emerge as indirect consequences of  
recursively compatible local economies. Order, function, and  
value would not be imposed from above, but licensed by the  
way possibilities can least costfully cohere.

“ Good is not the goal.  
It is what remains  
when low-cost order  
is given room  
to become.

Minimizing action is not the same as maximizing good.  
Yet under the right order-theoretic and dynamical conditions,  
a world engineered for low action may recursively produce  
stable, intelligible, and even desirable forms.

THE WORLD-ENGINEERING PROCESS (RECAP)

distinction

order  
(poset)

adjacency  
(cover graph)

local action  
rule  
{ L(x_i,x_j) }

path ensemble  
(competition)

x_T  
low-action selection

realization ∃j  
emergent good

The diagrams combine posets, cover graphs, path trajectories, contour maps, and emergent “good” symbols to show how local least-action rules scale into ordered structures or fail through hidden coupling. The palette is restrained but high-contrast, with black condensed type, deep red emphasis, teal headers, blue geometric accents, and a worn print texture.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 1 / 9
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 1 / 9

CAN GOD BE JUST  
IF NATURE IS UNCARING?

Setting the problem: how can divine justice coexist with a world  
whose local dynamics do not visibly care?

α  
Ω

[illegible]  
[illegible]

G  
GOODNESS  
G(x_T)  
Intrinsic value of  
flourishing realized  
in outcomes.  
Scale: real-valued  
Aim: maximize good

S  
ACTION  
S[p]  
Action (cost) of a path p:  
the natural “effort” or  
resistance encountered.  
Scale: ≥ 0  
Aim: minimize action

J  
JUSTICE  
J(x_0 → x_T)  
Righteous order across  
persons, times, and  
outcomes.  
Scale: real-valued  
Aim: uphold justice

The quantities. Different logics. Possible tension.

LOCAL NATURE: UNCARING MECHANISMS

IMPARTIAL FLOWS  
No preference.  
No concern.

COLLISIONS  
Energy redistributed  
without regard.

SELECTION  
Filters outcomes  
not values.

DECAY & NOISE  
Entropy grows.  
Order erodes.

LAW-FOLLOWING  
Transitions by law,  
not compassion.

Local rules are lawful, stable, and indifferent.  
They answer: “What happens?” not “What ought to happen?”

GLOBAL PROVIDENCE: ORDERING FRAME

CONSTRAIN  
Boundary conditions  
limit the possible.

REDIRECT  
Guide flows  
away from harm.

REPAIR  
Restore, redeem,  
or compensate.

WEIGHT  
Weight goods  
and wrongs.

JUDGE  
Uphold justice  
across the whole.

A higher-order architecture can shape the arena,  
select among histories, and heal what went wrong.

THE SPACE OF POSSIBLE HISTORIES

State space X

All possible states  
of the world

Nodes = states x  
Edges = lawful  
transitions

A path p = {x_0,...,x_T}

LEGEND  
Least-action paths  
Other possible paths  
High-action paths  
Moral gold (high G)

x_0  
x_T

There are infinitely many ways from x_0 to x_T.  
Least-action paths are dynamically “easy,”  
but not automatically morally best.

ACTION OF A PATH

S[p] = ∫_0^T L(x(t), ẋ(t), t) dt

The action accumulates local  
“difficulty” along the path.

LEAST-ACTION PRINCIPLE

p* = arg min_p S[p]

Nature tends to realize paths  
near p*, not because they are  
good, but because they are easy.

Easy ≠ Good | Easy ≠ Just

p* = arg min_p S[p] | Goodness ≠ low action | Justice ≠ mere efficiency

THE PROBLEM

Least action  
explains what is easy.

Justice asks  
what is right.

CORE QUESTION

Can a just order  
be real if the  
immediate mechanism  
is blind?

FOUR WAYS TO VIEW THE WORLD

PRIMARY  
FOCUS

OUTCOMES

PROCESSES

1  
UNCARING LAWS  
(ontology of nature)  
Laws operate without regard  
to value. Outcomes are products  
of impersonal mechanisms.

2  
CARING OUTCOMES  
(final-state goodness)  
Some outcomes exhibit goodness  
and flourishing, but the process  
that produces them is blind.

4  
INDIFFERENT PROCESS  
(brute mechanism)  
Processes run without intention  
or telos. No built-in concern  
for persons or meaning.

3  
JUST GOVERNANCE  
(moral ordering)  
A governing order evaluates,  
directs, and upholds justice  
across the whole.

INSTRUMENTALLY NEUTRAL → NORMATIVE STANDING

LIVE HYPOTHESES

1  
GOD MAXIMIZES GOOD DIRECTLY.  
Divine will aims at G; action is a  
means among others.

2  
GOD MINIMIZES ACTION AND  
GOOD EMERGES INDIRECTLY.  
Nature follows least action; good  
arises as a by-product.

3  
GOD COMPOSES BOTH  
OBJECTIVES.  
Justice emerges from a coherent  
optimization over G, S, and J.

4  
NATURE IS UNCARING LOCALLY  
BUT GOVERNED GLOBALLY.  
Local mechanisms are blind; a  
higher order secures justice.

HOW WE WILL EVALUATE THEORIES

GOODNESS G  
Does the view allow real  
good and flourishing?  
Measure: expected G  
across histories

ACTION S[p]  
Does the view respect the  
structure of the world?  
Measure: total action  
or complexity

JUSTICE J  
Does the view ground  
righteous order?  
Measure: justice  
across persons & time

SYMBOLS & MOTIFS

Halo geometry — Transcendent order  
World network — Space of states  
Scales — Justice in balance  
Bridge — Repair & redemption  
Gold star — Moral goods

WORKING THESIS FOR THIS SERIES

This series explores whether goodness and action can be composed  
without collapsing one into the other.

The layout moves from top definitions of goodness, action, and justice into paired mechanism/providence panels, then a large network diagram of possible histories with highlighted paths and moral “gold” stars. Lower panels frame the philosophical problem through a quadrant chart, live hypotheses, evaluation criteria, and recurring symbols such as trees, scales, bridges, halos, and state-space networks.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 2 / 9
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 2 / 9

THE AUTODIDACTIC UNIVERSE

Integrating arXiv:2104.03902: what if nature learns its laws,  
but does not directly learn goodness?

α  
Ω

η  
α

AUTODIDACTIC

A system that learns  
without external  
supervision.

THEORY-SPACE: A LANDSCAPE OF POSSIBLE LAWS

Many possible  
world-models  
(laws)

High predictive  
power / stability

Low predictive  
power / stability

Our world:  
a small, stable  
island in law-space

KEY CORRESPONDENCE

MATRIX  
MODELS

↔

LARGE-N  
MATRICES

GAUGE /  
GRAVITY  
THEORY

↔

RETICULAR  
GEOMETRY /  
EMERGENCE

RECURRENT  
OR CYCLIC  
NEURAL  
NETWORK

↔

LEARNING  
SYSTEM  
(AUTODIDACTIC)

PROTOCOLS IN THE PAPER

1 Renormalization-  
group learning

Coarse-grain, compare,  
and update to improve  
predictive performance  
across scales.

2 Precedence

New laws or effective  
rules can emerge only  
from what precedes them.

3 Self-sampling

The system draws  
its own experiences  
from its current model.

4 Variety

Maintain diverse  
hypotheses/models  
to avoid premature  
convergence.

5 Geometric  
self-assembly

Structure and locality  
emerge from interaction  
rules and feedback.

HIERARCHY OF LEVELS: STABLE EFFECTIVE LAWS MAY ARISE AT EACH LEVEL

MICRO

Fundamental  
degrees of  
freedom

Effective laws  
at the micro  
level

MESO

Collective  
structures  
& patterns

Effective laws  
at the meso  
level

MACRO

Phenomena  
& observable  
regularities

Effective laws  
at the macro  
level

At each level, unsupervised adaptation can stabilize models that predict well.  
These are laws-for-us, not promises-for-our-good.

DOES LAW-LEARNING  
IMPLY VALUE-LEARNING?

Law-learning  
(is)

≠

Value-learning  
(ought)

NOT  
NECESSARILY.

TENSION: LEARNING VS GOODNESS

LEARNING (is)

What is optimized:  
• Fit to experience  
• Stability across time & scale  
• Compression of information  
• Predictive success  
• Reusable structure

ADAPTIVE SUCCESS  
IS NOT MORAL WORTH.

GOODNESS (ought)

What is pursued:  
• Flourishing of creatures  
• Justice & right order  
• Mercy, fidelity, covenant  
• Protection of the weak  
• Good ends for persons

IMPLICATION FOR THEOLOGY

1 A learning universe may be lawful  
without being compassionate.

2 Providence may govern a world whose  
immediate learning rule is blind.

3 Justice may have to be imposed,  
composed, or recovered at a  
higher level.

AUTODIDACTIC LOOP (THE CORE IDEA)

Propose / Maintain  
Models (Variety)

Self-sample  
Experiences

Evaluate Fit  
Across Scales

Coarse-Grain  
(RG Step)

Update /  
Reweight  
Models

Unsupervised  
adaptation  
improves  
predictive  
power.

MINI FORMAL SKETCH

Model space: \(\mathcal{M}\)  
Data space: \(\mathcal{D}\)

\(m_t \in \mathcal{M}\)  
self-sample  
\(d_t \sim P_{m_t}(\mathcal{D})\)

score  
\(\rightarrow S(m_t, d_t) \rightarrow\) update

\(m_{t+1} \leftarrow U(m_t, d_t)\)

Objective (informal):  
maximize long-run utility  
of prediction across scales.

\(\max_m \mathbb{E}[U(({S(m,d_{1:T})})_{\text{scales}})]\)  
(unsupervised)

THESIS

AUTODIDACTIC NATURE GIVES US ADAPTIVE LAW, NOT YET MORAL LAW.

Law may be learned. Goodness must be chosen, bestowed, or discovered higher.

WORKING THESIS FOR THIS SERIES

This series explores whether goodness and action can be composed  
without collapsing one into the other.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 3 / 9
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 3 / 9

HOW DO WE COMPOSE  
GOOD AND ACTION?

Multi-objective architectures for goodness G, action S[p], and justice J.

α  
Ω  
η  
α

1) SPACE OF POSSIBLE HISTORIES

Low-action path (eff.)  
High-good path  
Just path (different)  
Other paths

x_0 start  
x_T end

x_0  
x_T

Histories p are paths from x_0 to x_T.  
They differ in goodness G[p], action cost S[p], and justice J[p].

2) GOODNESS–ACTION TRADEOFF

Goodness  
G

Infeasible  
(ruled out by  
physics, logic,  
or constraints)

Pareto  
frontier  
(efficient  
histories)

Feasible region  
(achievable  
histories)

Lower is better  
for S (cost)

Lower is better for S (cost) →

Action cost  
S[p]

COMPOSITION SCHEMES

#  
SCHEME  
FORMAL RULE (for a history p)  
INTUITIVE MEANING  
PROMISE  
FAILURE MODE

1  
maximize  
G directly  
p* = arg max_p G[p]  
Choose the history  
with the greatest  
goodness.  
Direct moral aim;  
no dilution.  
May require extreme or  
wasteful action;  
ignores justice.

2  
minimize  
S[p] directly  
p* = arg min_p S[p]  
Choose the most  
efficient (least-action)  
history.  
Parsimony;  
conserves resources.  
Can yield little good;  
may tolerate injustice.

3  
minimize S[p]  
then maximize G  
Let P_min = arg min_p S[p]  
p* = arg max_{p ∈ P_min} G[p]  
Among the most efficient  
options, choose the most  
good.  
Efficiency first;  
tie-break by good.  
Good is constrained  
by an overly tight  
efficiency filter.

4  
maximize G  
then minimize S[p]  
Let P_max = arg max_p G[p]  
p* = arg min_{p ∈ P_max} S[p]  
Among the best goods,  
choose the least costly  
way.  
Protects high good;  
adds efficiency  
as tie-breaker.  
If many near-tied goods  
exist, may still be  
costly overall.

5  
maximize  
G − λS[p]  
p* = arg max_p {G[p] − λS[p]}  
λ > 0 is a weight.  
Trade goodness  
against action by  
a chosen weight.  
Simple, tunable;  
traces points on the  
Pareto frontier.  
Requires choosing λ;  
one weight fits none  
across all cases.

6  
minimize S[p]  
subject to G ≥ g_0  
p* = arg min_p S[p]  
subject to G[p] ≥ g_0.  
Achieve at least a  
target goodness with  
minimal cost.  
Guarantees a goodness  
floor; efficient among  
acceptable goods.  
If g_0 too high, may be  
infeasible or very  
expensive.

7  
maximize G  
subject to S[p] ≤ s_0  
p* = arg max_p G[p]  
subject to S[p] ≤ s_0.  
Within a cost budget,  
get the most good.  
Respects hard limits  
(resources, time,  
capacity).  
Budget may be too  
small to achieve  
meaningful good.

8  
joint optimization  
over (G, S, J)  
p* = arg max_p U(G[p], −S[p], J[p])  
(with Pareto or utility U)  
Evaluate and balance  
all three objectives  
at once.  
Handles tradeoffs  
explicitly; flexible  
and principled.  
Complex; needs  
transparent U and  
data on J.

KEY DISTINCTION

G  
J  
S[p]

Efficient is not identical to good,  
and good is not identical to just.

WHERE JUSTICE ENTERS

AS A WEIGHT  
Add J[p] with a weight μ.  
Maximize G − λS + μJ.  
Justice influences the balance.

AS A HARD CONSTRAINT  
Require J[p] ≥ J_0 (or satisfy  
constraints C_J[p]). Only just  
histories are admissible.

AS A PENALTY FOR  
EXTERNALIZED HARM  
Subtract μH_ext[p] where H_ext  
measures harm shifted to others  
or future persons.

AS A FINAL EVALUATOR  
OF HISTORIES  
First pick by G,S; then prefer the  
history with higher J[p] among  
equally good & efficient options.

Justice can shape the set, the score, or the final choice.

MAP OF THE COMPOSITION SPACE

Goodness  
G

Action cost  
S (low is better)

Justice  
J

Interpretation  
Each interior point  
weights G, S, and J  
in some way.  
Different rules pick  
different regions  
or points.  
There is no single  
correct corner.

ALGORITHMIC VIEW (ABSTRACTION)

Define world  
& admissible  
histories P

Score histories  
(G[p], S[p], J[p])  
Measure & model

Choose rule  
(1–8 above)  
Set parameters

Select p* by chosen composition rule

Act along p*

Reassess & learn  
Update measures  
& rule if needed

Composition is the real problem—not whether action or goodness exists, but how they are ordered.

Theological plausibility lives in the composition rule.

Measure carefully. Choose transparently. Remain answerable.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 4 / 9
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 4 / 9

α  
Ω

TROPICAL ALGEBRA  
OF PROVIDENCE

η  
α

Using min-plus and max-plus composition to think  
about action, value, and path selection.

TROPICAL SEMIRING  
(MIN-PLUS)

a ⊕ b = min(a, b)

a ⊗ b = a + b

SEMIRING TABLES (MIN-PLUS)

a ⊕ b = min(a, b)

| ⊕ | 0 | 1 | 2 | ∞ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| ∞ | 0 | 1 | 2 | ∞ |

∞ is additive identity: a ⊕ ∞ = a  
0 is additive absorbing: a ⊕ 0 = 0

a ⊗ b = a + b

| ⊗ | 0 | 1 | 2 | ∞ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | ∞ |
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 3 | ∞ |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 4 | ∞ |
| ∞ | ∞ | ∞ | ∞ | ∞ |

0 is multiplicative identity: a ⊗ 0 = a  
∞ is multiplicative absorbing: a ⊗ ∞ = ∞

DUAL MAX-PLUS VIEW  
(MAX-PLUS)

a ⊕ b = max(a, b)

a ⊗ b = a + b

PATH COMPOSITION: ADD ALONG, MIN OVER ALTERNATIVES

Local action terms add  
along a path (⊗ = +).

Path selection happens  
by min (⊕ = min).

Start  
Goal  
Edge (x → y)  
with local cost L(x, y)

x₀  
a  
b  
c  
xT

L(x₀, a) = 2  
L(x₀, b) = 1  
L(x₀, c) = 3  
L(a, xT) = 3  
L(b, xT) = 4  
L(c, xT) = 2

Path costs (additive):  
x₀ → a → xT : 2 + 3 = 5  
x₀ → b → xT : 1 + 4 = 5  
x₀ → c → xT : 3 + 2 = 5

Path selection (min):  
V(x₀, xT) = min{5, 5, 5} = 5

General path value:  
V(x₀, xT) = min_{π ∈ P(x₀,xT)} Σ_{k=0}^{T−1} L(x_k, x_{k+1})

BELLMAN RECURSION  
(TROPICAL DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING)

V(x) = min_y [ L(x, y) + V(y) ]

V(x) is the least action-to-go from x.  
Terminal condition: V(x_terminal) = 0.  
Compute V by iterating until convergence.

WHY THIS MATTERS HERE

LEAST-ACTION SEARCH  
is naturally min-plus.  
We ADD costs along  
paths and take the  
MIN over alternatives.

GOODNESS AGGREGATION  
may look max-like  
or threshold-like.

threshold

MIN-PLUS vs. MAX-PLUS INTUITION

Min-plus (least action)  
• Lower is better  
• Costs accumulate  
• Selection is min

Max-plus (goodness)  
• Higher is better  
• Values accumulate  
• Selection is max

In providential reasoning, both may appear. Frame clearly which semiring governs which layer.

COMPOSING MORAL AND DYNAMICAL QUANTITIES WITH TROPICAL IDEAS

COST PENALTIES  
(additive in path)  
Add penalties to local cost.  
They accumulate.

L′(x, y) = L(x, y) + P(x, y)

JUSTICE PENALTIES  
(weighted costs)  
Encode injustice as cost.  
Heavier = steer away.

L′(x, y) = L(x, y) + J(x, y)

GOODNESS FLOORS  
(threshold constraints)  
Require goodness ≥ τ  
or pay a large penalty.

L′(x, y) = L(x, y) +  
{ 0 if G(x, y) ≥ τ  
  M if G(x, y) < τ }

LEXICOGRAPHIC MINIMA  
(ordered priorities)  
Minimize in layers:  
primary before secondary.

Primary (e.g., justice)  
⊕  
Secondary (e.g., cost)  
⊕  
Tertiary (e.g., ease)

SOFT CONSTRAINTS  
(soft weights)  
Soften constraints via  
finite penalties.

L′(x, y) = L(x, y) + w · c(x, y)  
w ≥ 0

All are min-plus compositions. Only the design of L′ and the constraint scheme changes what the search prefers.

THREE ARCHITECTURES FOR ACTION SELECTION

① PURE LEAST-ACTION  
Minimize total cost.

V(x) = min_y [ L(x, y) + V(y) ]

Seeks the easiest path.  
Blind to value unless  
encoded as cost.

② GOODNESS-WEIGHTED  
LEAST-ACTION

Minimize cost, but encode  
goodness as negative cost  
or floor/bonus.

L′(x, y) = L(x, y) − λ · G(x, y)  
V(x) = min_y [ L′(x, y) + V(y) ]

Rewards good outcomes  
while still counting cost.

③ JUSTICE-CONSTRAINED  
LEAST-ACTION

Minimize cost subject to  
justice constraints.

J(x, y) ≤ κ (hard)  
or  
L′(x, y) = L(x, y) + J(x, y)  
(soft)

V(x) = min_y [ L′(x, y) + V(y) ]

Protects justice first,  
then seeks least action  
within allowable space.

WARNING

Tropical elegance does  
not solve the ethical  
problem by itself.

It only clarifies  
composition.

PROVIDENTIAL READING

God may set the algebra of selection:

WHAT ADDS  
Which quantities  
accumulate along  
the way.

WHAT COMPETES  
Which alternatives  
confront each other  
at choices.

WHAT IS ALLOWED  
TO WIN  
Which constraints  
can veto, cap,  
or permit.

Not all values are of the  
same kind. The algebra  
we choose is already  
a moral decision.

THESIS

Tropical algebra gives a language for composing  
ease, value, and constraint.
posters.visual: A tall, ornate dark infographic uses gold/copper borders, teal accents, and classical serif/small-cap typography. The layout stacks panels from definitions and semiring tables at the top, through a central path graph and Bellman recursion, into moral constraint variants, action-selection architectures, warnings, and a thesis footer. Node-link networks, arrows, threshold curves, justice scales, compass-like ornaments, and theological iconography visually connect algebraic path selection with ethical and providential framing.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 5 / 9
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 5 / 9

WHEN DOES LOW ACTION  
YIELD GOOD?

The conditional emergence of goodness from least-action architectures.

α  
Ω

η  
α

GOODNESS  
EMERGES

Not all low-action paths are good. Goodness emerges only when structure pre-conditions the landscape.

ORDER  
OVER  
CHAOS

Least action flows most easily along stable, protected channels to stable, beneficial attractors.

JUSTICE  
AS DESIGN

Justice arranges the world so that what is cheap to do is also good to arrive at.

CONDITIONAL PRINCIPLE: Least action can yield good — but only under structural conditions.

ORDERED WORLD-SYSTEM: LOW-ACTION CHANNELS TO GOOD REGIONS

DISORDERED  
REGIONS

PROTECTED  
CHANNELS  
low action paths

STABLE GOOD  
REGIONS  
(flourishing attractors)

Low-action trajectory  
High-action / costly path  
Barrier / protection  
Attractor (good state)

Properly structured landscapes guide least-action  
flows toward good attractors.

GOODNESS EMERGES WHEN...

1  
STABLE INTERFACES  
Boundaries that persist and mediate interaction reliably.

2  
WEAK HIDDEN COUPLING  
Distant parts do not strongly destabilize one another.

3  
SCALE SEPARATION  
Fast processes settle locally; slow structure guides the whole.

4  
PROTECTED SUBSPACES  
Low-action routes are shielded from disruptive perturbations.

5  
OPEN RESERVOIRS  
Dissipation of Disorder  
Energy and entropy can flow out; disorder does not accumulate.

6  
ALIGNMENT BETWEEN  
LOCAL MINIMA AND  
GLOBAL GOODS  
What is cheap to reach is also truly good for the whole.

LOW-ACTION PATHS: WHEN IT WORKS — AND WHEN IT FAILS

WHEN IT WORKS  
Protected channels lead to flourishing.

Protected paths  
Healthy dissipation  
Aligned attractors  
Stable order

FLOURISHING  
GOOD STATES  
Life, virtue, knowledge,  
mutual benefit.

VS.

WHEN IT FAILS  
Cheap paths lead to cheap, but poor ends.

CHEAP BUT  
POOR STATES  
Exploitation, stagnation,  
disharmony, decay.

Leaky boundaries  
Hidden coupling  
No scale separation  
Misaligned minima  
Disorder trapped

INDIRECT EMERGENCE

Good need not be explicitly targeted if the landscape is structured so that cheap lawful trajectories terminate in genuinely good regions.

FROM THE AUTODIDACTIC-UNIVERSE PAPER

Learning and adaptation can discover stable, usable law from regularities in feedback. Such law can yield predictability, coordination, and thriving ecosystems.

But stability is not morality: an autodidactic process converges on what works, not necessarily on what is just.

QUESTION: WHO ALIGNS  
THE LANDSCAPE?

Providence / intentional design?  
Selection through evolution of worlds?  
Deep mathematical structure?  
All of the above?

THE STRUCTURAL FORMULA OF EMERGENT GOOD

ACTION MINIMIZATION  
Systems follow paths of least cost.

+

ALIGNED LANDSCAPE  
The world is structured so cheap paths lead toward good attractors.

+

PROTECTED ORDER  
Order, boundaries, and dissipation preserve the routes to good.

=

EMERGENT GOODS  
Flourishing, justice, knowledge, and beauty arise without direct micromanagement.

Conditional Principle Restated: Least action yields good only when the world is already arranged so that lawful ease and true good coincide.

DIAGNOSTIC QUESTIONS

ABOUT INTERFACES  
Are boundaries clear, stable, and permeable in the right ways?

ABOUT INFORMATION FLOWS  
Is information mostly local, accurate, and not hijacked by hidden coupling?

ABOUT SCALES  
Do fast and slow dynamics separate enough to allow coherent order?

ABOUT OUTLETS  
Can disorder, waste, and entropy exit instead of accumulating inside?

PRACTICAL IMPLICATION

Build institutions, norms, and technologies that create the conditions above, so that ordinary, self-interested, low-action choices collectively produce good.

SERIES THESIS

Least action yields good only when the world has already been prepared to make goodness dynamically reachable.
posters.visual: A tall illuminated-manuscript-style infographic uses gold serif typography on a dark star-chart background, organized into bordered panels, diagrams, and formula blocks. The central image shows glowing trajectory lines moving through protected channels from chaotic regions toward luminous tree-like “good” attractors, while the right column lists structural conditions with numbered icons. The visual language combines cosmology, systems diagrams, sacred geometry, justice scales, shields, and trees to frame moral emergence as a landscape of constrained dynamics.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 6 / 9
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 6 / 9

WHERE THE  
COMPOSITION FAILS

How low action can coexist with suffering,  
externalized cost, and injustice.

α  
Ω

η  
α

THE DIAGNOSTIC MAP

Least action (or low action) is not  
a moral guarantee. These failure  
modes explain how unjust,  
harming, or indifferent outcomes  
can still be dynamically cheap.

Look for:  
★ Invisible costs  
★ Bypass of the good  
★ Dispossession & dumping  
★ Historical lock-in  
★ Scale mismatch  
★ Indifference to persons

FAILURE MODES

1  
LOCAL MINIMA VS GLOBAL GOOD  
The system settles in an easy basin  
that blocks access to better states.

2  
HIDDEN INTERACTION COSTS  
Costs are pushed into weakly coupled  
parts of the system and stay unseen.

3  
EXTERNALIZED SUFFERING  
Harm is displaced into reservoirs  
of persons or ecosystems.

4  
PATH DEPENDENCE / HYSTERESIS  
Early choices constrain the future;  
returning to the good becomes costly.

5  
NON-ADAPTIVITY ACROSS SCALES  
Cheap actions at one scale create  
expensive effects at another.

6  
INDIFFERENT SELECTION AMONG PERSONS  
Persons are treated as interchangeable  
tokens; who bears the cost is ignored.

MORAL WARNING

What is cheap  
for the system  
may be costly  
for persons.

EFFICIENCY VS JUSTICE

Two ways to measure  
the same world.

SYSTEM-LEVEL EFFICIENCY  
• Minimizes total action  
• Aggregates over parts  
• Ignores distribution  
• Silent on persons  
• Can stabilize harm

PERSON-LEVEL JUSTICE  
• Protects persons  
• Counts who is burdened  
• Respects rights & dignity  
• Seeks fair opportunity  
• Demands repair

STATE-SPACE DIAGRAM: CHEAP PATHS THAT FAIL

Cheap path (low action)  
Morally valuable path  
Cost dumping / suffering flow  
Attractor

Starting  
conditions

Cheap Attractor  
(dynamically stable)

Moral valley  
(harder path)

Bypass & Dump  
(externalized cost)

Reservoir of  
Suffering  
(people, places,  
ecologies)

The cheap path avoids morally valuable states  
and displaces cost into a reservoir of suffering.

LEARNING WITHOUT VALUE  
(From the Autodidactic-Universe Lens)

Learning optimizes what is usable —  
information that increases stability,  
predictability, or control.

But it can remain blind to value.

DATA

STABLE  
PREDICTIONS

This system learns what works,  
not what is right.

EFFECTIVE ACTION THAT IGNORES HARMS

If the accounting of action omits some harms,  
the minimizer can be unjust.

Let real costs = C_real = C_sys + C_ignored

System counts:  
S = ∫_a^b L_sys(x, ẋ) dt  
Minimize S

But true moral cost:  
S_true = ∫_a^b [L_sys(x, ẋ) + L_harm(x, ẋ)] dt  
Minimize S_true

If L_harm is ignored, arg min S may cause large L_harm.

Therefore: Minimizing the counted action does not ensure justice.

WHY UNCARING NATURE MATTERS

Because blind lawful adaptation can  
stabilize structures that are dynamically  
efficient yet morally unacceptable.

Blind  
Adapter

Lawful  
Dynamics

Stable  
Structure

• Stability without conscience is not justice.  
• Nature does not aim at the good.  
• Only persons can recognize & respond to it.

WHAT TO LOOK FOR

★ Cheap paths that bypass the good  
★ Hidden & displaced costs  
★ People or places used as sinks  
★ Lock-in that preserves unjust states  
★ Winners at one scale, losers at another  
★ Indifference to who suffers  
★ No mechanism for repair or restitution

DIAGNOSTIC QUESTIONS

① Who (or what) pays the real cost?  
② What options were foreclosed early?  
③ What is made invisible by aggregation?  
④ Is the path reversible without great harm?  
⑤ Does this scale up without injustice?  
⑥ Are persons treated as ends or means?

WHAT JUSTICE REQUIRES

★ Make all relevant costs visible  
★ Protect persons from being used  
★ Distribute burdens fairly  
★ Preserve options & reversibility  
★ Align efficiency with human dignity  
★ Repair what was unjustly imposed  
★ Choose the path that is both wise & good

THE THESIS

A just theology cannot simply baptize the cheapest trajectory.  
It must judge the path by truth, dignity, and love—  
not merely by the economy of motion.
posters.visual: The poster is arranged as a dense illuminated-manuscript style infographic, with a large title at top, boxed diagnostic panels across the upper and lower sections, and a wide state-space diagram through the middle. It uses black parchment texture with antique gold borders and serif typography, accented by teal for “cheap” system paths and red/orange for suffering or cost-dumping flows. Diagrams include minima curves, networks, factories, persons, scales of justice, attractors, and cosmic/astronomical motifs, giving the argument both computational and theological symbolism.

posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 7 / 9
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES · 7 / 9

WHAT IF  
NEGATION IS GOOD?

Restraint, inhibition, and the moral ambiguity  
of minimizing harmful action.

α  
Ω

η  
ά

THREE MODES OF NEGATION

1  
GOOD  
NEGATION

Restraint that protects.

Restraint, nonviolence  
Inhibition of harmful  
transitions  
Refusal of cruelty  
Interruption of destructive  
cascades

2  
AMBIGUOUS  
NEGATION

Unclear motives,  
uncertain effects.

Passivity without reflection  
Quietism or withdrawal  
Moral delay  
Bureaucratic nonresponse  
Willful ignorance

3  
BAD  
NEGATION

Neglect that harms.

Suppression of flourishing  
Refusal of aid  
Paralysis in the face  
of suffering  
Neglect that enables  
injustice

NEGATION \neq NIHILISM.

To refrain from certain actions is not to deny value or responsibility.

TRAJECTORIES OF HARM AND THE MORAL POWER OF RESTRAINT

POTENTIAL  
HARMFUL CAUSES

Violence  
Cruel Policy  
Deception  
Exploitation  
Ecological  
Harm

A. BLOCKED  
Wise inhibition  
prevents harm.

B. DAMPED  
Proportionate restraint  
reduces harm.

C. REDIRECTED  
Restraint channels  
toward good.

GOOD OUTCOMES  
PRESERVED

Human Dignity  
Flourishing  
Communities  
Trust & Justice  
Sustainable  
Systems  
Knowledge & Truth

D. INDISCRIMINATE SUPPRESSION

All action shut down.  
Good and bad alike wither.

Blanket passivity  
erases goods and  
enables evil.

Stagnation • Suffering  
Injustice • Collapse

THE REAL QUESTION

Which actions should  
be prevented, and at  
whose expense?

THE SIGNED COST

Harmful action  
is pruned.

Compassionate  
intervention is  
preserved.

Not all restraint is wise.  
Not all action is good.  
Discernment signs the cost.

JUSTICE FRAME

Restraint is good only when it:

Protects persons from harm  
Prevents wrongdoing  
Preserves the conditions  
of flourishing  
Respects the dignity and  
agency of persons affected  
Is proportionate and revisable

A NECESSARY WARNING

Pure passivity can  
become complicity.

When we could prevent  
wrong and will not,  
we tacitly consent.

GUIDING DISCERNMENT

Know the ends.  
Seek the good  
and avoid evil.

Examine causes.  
Understand what  
your restraint enables  
or prevents.

Consider persons.  
Who is helped?  
Who is harmed?

Choose proportion.  
Least force.  
Most good.

Remain answerable.  
Reassess. Revise.  
Repair.

THESIS

Negation is morally meaningful only when it is  
selective, protective, and answerable to justice.

The top third compares three circular “modes of negation” beside a living green branch and a dead red branch; the middle uses flow-line diagrams to show harmful causes being blocked, damped, redirected, or indiscriminately suppressed. Lower panels use symbolic figures such as scales, a question mark, warning triangle, eye-in-triangle, arrows, and checklist marks to frame moral discernment, responsibility, and justice.
