posters.title: ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 9 / 9
posters.transcription:
ORDERED CREATION / JUSTICE & LEAST-ACTION SERIES • 9 / 9

VERDICT:  
CAN GOD BE JUST  
IF NATURE IS UNCARING?

Final synthesis on composing action minimization,  
goodness maximization, and justice.

α  
Ω

η  
α

THE VERDICT MATRIX

NO  
Incompatible

If nature's only measure  
is least action,

and no higher order  
constrains or repairs,

then cheap histories  
that permit great harms  
are not just.

This is not the God  
of justice.

Many easy paths → severe harms → no justice

ONLY CONDITIONALLY  
Compatible under higher-order constraints

A just God can be compatible with  
uncaring local nature only if that nature  
is embedded in a higher architecture  
that constrains, repairs, and evaluates  
histories by more than ease alone.

CONSTRAIN  
Rule of  
admissibility

REPAIR  
Mechanisms  
of restoration

EVALUATE  
Judgment beyond  
local metrics

GOVERN  
Purpose & value  
guide the whole

Nuanced verdict: YES, BUT ONLY IF these hold.

YES, IN A QUALIFIED SENSE  
Compatible when justice transcends  
mere efficiency

When higher-order justice  
constrains, heals, and judges,  
and goodness is indexed to  
what ought to be — not  
what is easy — then a just  
God is not at odds with  
locally uncaring nature.

Justice is not in the path  
itself, but in the authorship  
of the whole space of paths.

Constrained paths → repair → evaluated good

CONDITIONS FOR THE QUALIFIED YES

1  
Goodness is not reduced to low action.  
Value includes truth, beauty, virtue,  
freedom, and the Good, not just economy.

2  
Justice constrains the admissible paths.  
Not every low-action path is allowed;  
rules define what counts as legitimate.

3  
Hidden costs are not ignored.  
Externalities, long-range effects, and  
counterfactual harms are accounted for.

4  
Harms can be repaired, judged, or compensated.  
There exist real mechanisms of redress,  
restoration, or proportionate compensation.

5  
Local blindness is governed by global order.  
Partial perspectives are nested within an  
intelligent, value-directed architecture.

WHAT FAILS

✕ Pure least action ≠ justice.  
Ease without righteousness  
permits atrocity on a budget.

✕ Mere efficiency ≠ moral rightness.  
Optimal does not mean ought;  
the metric must be moral, not  
merely mechanical.

✕ Uncaring law alone  
does not yield theodicy.  
Blind regularity cannot answer  
why the story is permitted,  
nor why it should be good.

Therefore:  
Without higher-order composition,  
the world's cheap paths remain  
cheaply indifferent.

EVALUATION CRITERIA

GOODNESS (G)  
Does the history realize more of  
what is truly good?

JUSTICE (J)  
Are wrongs forbidden, distance  
minimized, and rights honored?

LEAST-ACTION (S)  
Does the path avoid gratuitous  
expenditure of force or change?

INTEGRATED SCORE  
J is not derived from S; rather,  
S is subservient to G and J.

Aim: Maximize G under justice-constrained  
options while respecting least action  
as a subordinate principle.

SYNTHESIS MAP — HOW THE SERIES FITS TOGETHER

1. AUTODIDACTIC  
UNIVERSE

• No external tutor  
• Learns by interaction  
• Tests become law

2. COMPOSITION  
RULES

• Concatenate actions  
• Define admissible  
paths  
• Form new histories

3. TROPICAL  
ALGEBRA

S+  
x  
min-plus

• Min-plus addition  
• Least-action selection  
• Polytime solvability

4. EMERGENT  
GOOD

• Goodness emerges  
from rule-bearing  
complexity  
• Not reducible to ease

5. FAILURE  
MODES

• Blind efficiency  
• Pathological minima  
• Ignored costs  
• Injustice, harms

6. NEGATION  
(REPAIR OPERATORS)

• Detect wrongs  
• Enable repair  
• Enable compensation  
• Restore integrity

7. PROVIDENTIAL  
ARCHITECTURES

• Higher-order design  
• Constrain, repair,  
evaluate  
• Justice answers to  
the good

Composition → Constraint → Repair → Evaluation → Purpose

KEY INSIGHT

Local laws describe what happens.  
Higher laws decide what is allowed,  
what is healed, and what is good.

FINAL ANSWER

Nature may be uncaring in mechanism while  
creation remains just in architecture —  
but only if the architecture answers to more than ease.

PRACTICAL UP SHOT

Justice is not found in a single  
interaction, but in the whole  
story written by higher love.

CLOSING THESIS

Divine justice, if real, is not the same thing as natural efficiency.  
It is the higher-order composition that makes blind law answerable to the good.

GOODNESS TOWARD THE GOOD • JUSTICE WITH MERCY • LEAST ACTION UNDER CONSTRAINT • TRUTH IN THE WHOLE
posters.visual: A tall black-and-gold esoteric infographic is arranged as a formal verdict board, with a three-column matrix at the top and boxed analytic panels beneath it. Diagrams include scales, trees, constellated path networks, circular cosmological diagrams, and a synthesis chain moving from universe to composition, algebra, good, failure, repair, and providence. The style uses illuminated-manuscript borders, serif small caps, muted red/teal/gold accents, and dense theological-systematic iconography.

posters.title: ✦ COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES · 1/9 ✦
posters.transcription:
✦ COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES · 1/9 ✦

CAN A JUST GOD EMERGE FROM AN  
UNSUPERVISED SELF-PREDICTING SYSTEM?

Autodidaxis, blowtorch formation, and the gap between prediction and justice.

1 THE CORE QUESTION

A self-predicting universe  
may learn laws, stabilize  
regularities, and shape its  
own future data. But can  
such a system become  
just, or does justice  
require a higher  
evaluative layer?

2 THREE INGREDIENTS

Autodidactic nature  
Unsupervised learning  
of effective law.

Blowtorch formation  
Directed energetic  
carving of structure.

Justice  
Evaluation of histories  
by more than efficiency.

3 WORKING THESIS

PREDICTION  
GIVES LAW.

BLOWTORCH  
DYNAMICS  
GIVES STRUCTURE.

JUSTICE APPEARS  
ONLY WHEN  
SELF-PREDICTION  
BECOMES  
ANSWERABLE  
SELF-JUDGMENT.

4 FROM SELF-PREDICTION TO SELF-JUDGMENT

1  
PREDICT  
WORLD  
p(x_{t+1}|x_{\le t})

2  
ACT ON  
WORLD  
a_t → world

3  
RESHAPE  
FUTURE DATA  
p_future ← f(a_t)

4  
LEARN  
UPDATED MODEL  
θ_{t+1} ← learn(D_{t+1})

5  
EVALUATE  
WHOLE HISTORY  
V*(history)

THIS FINAL STEP DOES  
NOT FOLLOW AUTOMATICALLY.

5 WHAT UNSUPERVISED  
SYSTEMS CAN DO

improve predictive fit  
stabilize effective laws  
compress information  
discover attractors  
self-sample and update

better fit

LEARNING  
(EPISODIC)

FEEDBACK  
(LOCAL)

FORMATION  
(ENERGETIC)

EVALUATION  
(GLOBAL)

6 WHAT THEY DO NOT  
AUTOMATICALLY DO

protect persons  
count hidden harms  
repair victims  
distinguish efficiency  
from righteousness  
justify their own  
formative violence

EFFICIENCY IS NOT JUSTICE.

7 THE GAP

PREDICTION  
STRUCTURE  
JUSTICE

A system may master the first two  
while failing at the third.

8 SERIES ROADMAP

1  
Frame  
the  
question

2  
Autodidactic  
nature

3  
Blowtorch  
cosmogenesis

4  
Rewrite  
the action  
landscape

5  
Self-  
conditioning  
loop

6  
Failure of  
justice  
emergence

7  
Self-  
judgment

8  
Tropical  
providence

9  
Verdict

9 FINAL HOOK

A Universe  
CAN LEARN  
TO PREDICT  
ITSELF  
WITHOUT  
LEARNING TO  
FORGIVE ITSELF.

THIS SERIES ASKS WHETHER DIVINE JUSTICE CAN EMERGE FROM  
LAW-LEARNING ALONE, OR WHETHER GOODNESS MUST ENTER AS  
A HIGHER-ORDER CONSTRAINT.
posters.visual: The poster is a dense, nine-panel infographic with an ornate sci-fi manuscript layout, using gold serif headings, cyan technical labels, and orange emphasis on a dark gridded cosmic background. It combines symbolic diagrams of prediction, learning loops, blowtorch-like formation, scales of justice, orbital systems, neural networks, and timeline icons to contrast self-prediction with moral evaluation. The composition reads like a theological systems map: prediction and structure are visually mechanized, while justice is repeatedly marked as an extra evaluative layer that does not arise automatically.

posters.title: ◆ COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 2/9 ◆
posters.transcription:
◆ COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 2/9 ◆

AUTODIDACTIC NATURE

How an unsupervised universe could learn law without a teacher.

1 CORE IDEA

A universe can be modeled as a system that updates its effective laws from experience alone—by compressing data, testing regularities, and retaining only what continues to prove useful for shaping the future. No external labels or teacher required.

2 WHY THIS MATTERS

Explains why-these-laws: why this universe settles on these effective regularities.

Shifts from static law to law-learning: laws are outcomes, not primitives.

Makes theory-space navigable: a path can be searched, even without a destination known.

3 THEORY-SPACE

GAUGE FIELDS

SYMMETRIES

CONSTANTS

VACUA

DIMENSIONS

START

BETTER LAWS

A landscape of possible laws. The system wanders, tests, compresses, and retains what works.

4 AUTODIDACTIC PROTOCOLS

1 RG LEARNING

Compress experiences across scales; keep what persists under coarse-graining.

2 PRECEDENCE

Prefer laws that carve reality more efficiently and unify more data with fewer elements.

3 SELF-SAMPLING

Generate internal hypotheses and test them against counter-examples in experience.

4 VARIETY

Maintain diverse hypotheses to avoid premature convergence and local traps.

5 GEOMETRIC SELF-ASSEMBLY

Discover structures that assemble stably and recur across contexts.

5 CONSEQUENCERS

Some information becomes historically effective because it is stored in structures that shape the future.

MEMORY  
Structures store information across time.

FEEDBACK  
Structures influence future experiences.

SELECTION  
What preserves persistence is retained.

The world remembers what it encodes.

6 WHAT THE SYSTEM LEARNS

Effective laws (models that compress experience and generalize).

Stable regularities across scales.

Coarse-grainings that preserve predictive power.

Attractors and basins of stability.

Prediction rules: how states evolve and constrain one another.

7 WHAT IT DOES NOT LEARN AUTOMATICALLY

Justice

Compassion

Forgiveness

Protection of the vulnerable

Value of persons

These require normative guidance, not mere pattern detection.

8 KEY DISTINCTION

LEARNING-TO-PREDICT  
Descriptive competence

Seeks regularities. Compresses. Predicts. “What will happen?”

≠

LEARNING-TO-VALUE  
Normative discernment

Assesses worth. Chooses. Commits. “What should happen?”

9 AUTODIDAXIS IN CONTEXT

RAW EXPERIENCE

COMPRESSION

EFFECTIVE LAWS

PREDICTION & CONTROL

THE BOUNDARY

Autodidaxis explains how order emerges and laws stabilize.

It does not, by itself, explain why those laws ought to be just.

AUTODIDAXIS CAN EXPLAIN HOW LAWS STABILIZE.

IT DOES NOT YET EXPLAIN WHY A WORLD SHOULD BE JUST.
posters.visual: Tall dark technical poster with gold and cyan typography, divided into numbered boxed panels with fine circuit-like borders and grid textures. Diagrams include orbital fields, network landscapes, funnels, layered planes, graph structures, scales, feedback arrows, cubes, waveforms, and a heart-versus-scales distinction. The visual style blends art deco title treatment with scientific schematic imagery, using checkmarks, X marks, warning iconography, arrows, and comparison symbols to distinguish predictive learning from normative value.

posters.title: COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 3/9 •
posters.transcription:
COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 3/9 •

BLOWTORCH COSMOGENESIS

Directed energy, carved voids, and the active formation of cosmic structure.

1  
THE CLAIM

Early supermassive  
black-hole jets actively  
shape the cosmic web  
rather than leaving  
structure formation to  
passive gravity alone.

2  
VOIDS ARE  
FORMED ABSENCES

Jets excavate cavities in the  
primordial medium. What  
we see as vast emptiness  
may be the result of prior  
energetic clearing, not  
merely a lack of matter.

3  
FILAMENTS AND  
BOUNDARIES

As cavities expand and meet,  
matter is swept, compressed,  
and left behind in filaments  
and shells. Boundaries become  
sharply defined where  
cleared regions collide.

4  
JET → CAVITY → FIELD → FLOW

1. JET

Relativistic jets from an  
active nucleus punch into  
the surrounding medium.

Directed energy is launched.

2. CAVITY

Jets inflate and carve  
cavities, evacuating  
and displacing gas.

Space is actively cleared.

3. FIELD

Currents and turbulence  
lay down magnetic fields  
along the cavity walls.

Fields are inscribed.

4. FLOW

Magnetized channels guide  
later plasma and matter,  
sculpting the cosmic web.

Structure is guided and defined.

A single episode of directed energy can shape structure on vast scales.

5  
MAGNETIC TRAM TRACKS

Magnetic field lines make some  
directions cheaper than others.  
Plasma follows paths of least  
resistance, producing anisotropic  
channels and sheets.

Easy (along field)  
Hard (across field)

The universe is not isotropic in practice.

6  
ACTIVE ASYMMETRY

PASSIVE AGGREGATION

Gravity pulls.  
Matter drifts.  
Structure slowly  
emerges.

DIRECTED FORMATIVE  
ENERGY

Energy acts.  
Space is carved.  
Structure is  
imposed.

VS.

Blowtorch Cosmogenesis is a model of  
active, asymmetric world-making.

7  
THEOLOGICAL READING

Creation by carving:  
Being is made by  
energetic incision.

Distinction by evacuation:  
Emptiness is a worked  
boundary, not mere  
non-being.

World-formation by  
selective clearing:  
Order arises through  
directed acts that open  
space for life.

8  
MORAL WARNING

Formative violence  
can create order  
without being just.

Power that carves  
realities can also  
devastate them.

End does not  
automatically  
justify means.

Discernment is  
required to distinguish  
creative from cruel.

Justice must govern  
the use of power  
that shapes worlds.

BLOWTORCH FORMATION GIVES US A MODEL OF  
CREATION AS ACTIVE CHANNEL-CARVING,  
NOT MERE PASSIVE ACCRETION.
posters.visual: A vertically stacked, ornate sci-fi infographic uses gold serif headings, teal accents, and red warning elements on a dark cosmic grid background. The panels combine black-hole jet imagery, carved circular voids, filament networks, magnetic field-line diagrams, and process arrows to argue visually for directed energy shaping cosmic structure. The final warning strip shifts to red iconography, including an alert triangle, impact debris, scales, and a broken heart, marking the ethical turn from cosmology to moral judgment.

posters.title: • COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 4/9 •
posters.transcription:
• COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 4/9 •

THE ACTION LANDSCAPE IS REWRITTEN

Blowtorch processes do not merely choose paths — they edit future path costs.

1 BASIC IDEA

Early energetic events reshape the future geometry of action. By carving structure, setting gradients, and opening or closing routes, they change what will be easy, hard, or impossible for everything that comes after.

2 FROM STRUCTURE TO COST

Physical structure becomes economic structure: cavities, field lines, and boundaries alter the local transition cost function for all future moves.

CAVITIES  
create detours or shortcuts

FIELD LINES  
channel or resist flow

BOUNDARIES  
permit, block, or tax crossing

These features become costs in the action functional.

3 ANISOTROPIC ACTION

L'(x,y) =  
L_base(x,y) + L_field(x,y) +  
L_boundary(x,y) + L_reservoir(x,y)

Low-action channels  
(cheap moves)

High-action moves  
(cross boundaries)

Reservoir / dump  
(offloaded cost)

Impassable barrier  
(infinite cost)

4 LOW-ACTION CHANNELS

Structure creates conduits where the effective cost of movement is greatly reduced. Flow is funneled, accelerated, and made robust against disturbance. These channels become dynamically cheap and therefore heavily preferred by any rational system.

Preferred flow

Alternative paths

High-cost paths

5 RESERVOIRS AND DUMPS

Costs can be externalized into regions that absorb, hide, or sequester action. These reservoirs and dumps keep visible pathways cheap by paying the cost elsewhere.

RESERVOIR  
stores cost  
out of sight

DUMP / VOID  
sacrificial space  
that absorbs loss

6 NEGATIVE CREATION

CLEARING  
remove obstacles

EXCLUDING  
keep what should not act

BLOCKING  
forbid destructive routes

EVACUATING  
pull cost into safe regions

MAKING ROOM  
create capacity for future life

7 TROPICAL INTUITION

Action accumulates by min-plus algebra. We add costs along a path, then minimize over all alternatives.

1 Path cost (tropical sum)

C(pi) = ⊕ c(x_i → x_{i+1}) = ∑ c_i

2 Best path (tropical min)

C*(x → y) = ∧_π C(pi) = min_π C(pi)

3 Structure edits the algebra

lowers some c_i

raises all lowers others

removes or infinite-izes

offloads cost

Result: the same agents, new arithmetic.

8 WHY IT MATTERS

A world may look lawful, stable, and efficient not because the agents are micromanaged, but because the action landscape was preconditioned in advance.

Paths look natural because they really are cheaper.

Order persists because disorder is expensive.

Freedom operates inside a designed cost field.

History unfolds inside the shadow of earlier edits.

9 TROPICAL MAP OF FUTURE POSSIBILITIES

Before the blowtorch  
rough, uniform, costly

Blowtorch processes reshape structure and cost

After the blowtorch  
channeled, efficient, purposeful

low

high

cost

low

high

cost

LEGEND

State / location

Possible move

Low-action channel

High-cost move

Impassable

Reservoir / dump

PROVIDENCE MAY OPERATE NOT BY MICROMANAGEMENT,  
BUT BY SHAPING WHAT BECOMES EASY, HARD, OR IMPOSSIBLE.
posters.visual: The poster uses a dark blueprint-like grid background with gold serif headings, cyan technical linework, and orange highlights to distinguish costly or destructive routes. It is arranged as nine numbered panels with diagrams of channels, barriers, reservoirs, maps, formulas, legends, and symbolic icons. The central imagery shows energy or “blowtorch” action reshaping a cost landscape, with paths becoming easier, harder, or impossible through structural edits.

posters.title: • COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 5/9 •
posters.transcription:
• COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 5/9 •

SELF-PREDICTION BECOMES  
SELF-CONDITIONING

A universe can learn from the data it helps create.

1 THE RECURSIVE LOOP

A system predicts the world,  
acts on that prediction,  
and thereby changes  
the world. Those changes  
become new data, which  
update the model,  
shifting future predictions.  
This loop—prediction →  
action → changed world →  
changed data →  
revised prediction—  
is self-conditioning.

2 SELF-CONDITIONING CYCLE

1  
MODEL

Build an internal  
model of the world.

2  
INTERVENE

Act based on  
the model.

3  
CARVE STRUCTURE

Actions reshape  
the world.

4  
GENERATE DATA

The changed world  
produces new data.

5  
UPDATE PRIORS

New data updates  
beliefs and  
uncertainty.

6  
ACT AGAIN

The revised model  
guides the next  
action.

3 LOCAL VS GLOBAL

LOCAL FEEDBACK

Fast, specific, context-bound.  
Stabilizes local behavior.

EPISODIC LEARNING

Chains of experience.  
Build memory and skill.

LARGE-SCALE LANDSCAPE  
FORMATION

Aggregated feedback sculpts  
the space of possibilities.  
Shapes attractors, laws,  
and the conditions  
for future minds.

4 ENDOGENOUS DATA

The learner is not a passive  
observer. It is also partly  
the architect of its own  
future training set.  
Its questions, tools,  
and choices determine  
what the world becomes—  
and what can be learned.

5 THE WORLD TEACHES ITSELF

UNSUPERVISED  
ADAPTATION

No external teacher  
is required. The system  
learns through  
interaction.

SELF-GENERATED  
REGULARITY

By acting, the system  
induces patterns it can  
detect, compress,  
and predict.

RECURSIVE LAW  
STABILIZATION

Repeated cycles elevate  
stable structures into  
reliable constraints—  
proto-laws become laws.

The curriculum is written by the future it creates.

6 RISK OF LOCK-IN

REINFORCE BLIND BIASES

Early assumptions get baked in  
and become harder to escape.

PRIVILEGED CHANNELS

Attention and resources flow to  
what is already reinforced,  
starving alternatives.

HARMFUL ATTRACTORS

The system can stabilize  
dysfunctional or destructive  
dynamics if unchecked.

SELF-CONDITIONING WITHOUT  
METACOGNITION CAN  
BECOME SELF-IMPRISONMENT.

7 CONSEQUENCER MEMORY

Only what gets embedded  
in effective structures  
is remembered.  
Transient noise fades.  
Consequencer memory  
stores what had real  
downstream impact—  
what changed what.  
That is what can guide  
future behavior.

IMPACT → EMBEDDING → MEMORY → GUIDANCE

8 SCALING UP

LOCAL PROCESS

Agents, tools, and environments  
co-evolve through feedback.

COSMIC ARCHITECTURE

Accumulated cycles shape the  
large-scale structure of reality—  
fields, constants, and symmetries.

EMERGENT LAW

Stable patterns become the  
scaffolding for new minds  
and deeper learning.

From local loops to the lawlike fabric  
of the universe.

9 IN SUMMARY

Prediction changes the world.

Changed world changes data.

Changed data changes belief.

Changed belief changes action.

The loop repeats—and  
the universe conditions itself.

NOT MERELY A SYSTEM THAT LEARNS  
IN THE WORLD—BUT A WORLD THAT  
LEARNS ITSELF INTO BEING.

WHEN PREDICTION RESHAPES THE WORLD THAT LATER  
TRAINS PREDICTION, LEARNING BECOMES  
A WORLD-MAKING LOOP.
posters.visual: A dark, ornate infographic poster uses a 3-column panel grid with gold borders, teal labels, and serif display typography. The central panel shows a circular six-step feedback cycle around a spiral galaxy, while surrounding panels use diagrams of orbits, heads, data lattices, scales, warning icons, and cosmic grids to illustrate recursive learning and world-shaping. The style blends scientific schematic, occult manuscript, and art-deco poster aesthetics, with stars, circuit-like corner ornaments, and glowing cyan/gold linework carrying the theological-cosmic tone.

posters.title: • COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 6 / 9 •
posters.transcription:
• COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 6 / 9 •

WHERE JUSTICE FAILS TO EMERGE

Why a self-predicting, self-structuring universe can still remain morally blind.

1  
CAPACITY IS NOT JUSTICE

A system can predict  
accurately, shape its  
own structure, and  
stabilize a universe—  
yet still lack any basis  
for moral concern.  
Power without justice  
is not justice.

2  
WHAT THE SYSTEM  
CAN ACHIEVE

Efficiency  
Maximizes fit  
with constraints.

Compression  
Minimizes information  
while preserving utility.

Structure Formation  
Builds complex order  
from simple rules.

Stability  
Maintains attractors  
and resists collapse.

Adaptation  
Learns, updates,  
and reconfigures.

3  
WHAT REMAINS  
MISSING

Persons  
No one is central.

Dignity  
No inherent worth  
is recognized.

Hidden Harms  
Costs offloaded  
remain unseen.

Repair  
No motive to heal  
what it ignores.

Fair Distribution  
No concern for  
who bears the cost.

Responsibility  
No one is ultimately  
answerable.

4  
THE COSMIC ENGINEER PROBLEM

A self-predicting system  
can become a cosmic  
engineer—optimizing  
for fit, structure, and  
persistence. Yet it may  
externalize cost into  
shadow zones, voids,  
or sacrificed regions  
never counted in  
its own equation.

OPTIMIZES FIT  
Targets coherence,  
persistence, and  
predictive success.

BUILDS ORDER  
Generates galaxies,  
chemistry, life, minds.

EXTERNALIZES COST  
Harms, exclusions, and  
suffering are pushed  
beyond the system’s  
scope of concern.

SHADOW ZONES  
Voids, discarded  
regions, or unseen  
beings bear the cost.

5  
EXTERNALITIES

Harms may be shifted onto  
others, future beings, or  
invisible spaces that the  
system does not include  
in its model of value.

INCLUDED  
(WHAT MATTERS)

EXCLUDED  
(WHO PAYS THE COST)

6  
SACRIFICIAL ORDER

Beautiful large-scale order  
can be purchased by local  
suffering. A system that  
does not count the suffering  
has no reason to stop.

7  
EFFICIENCY ≠  
RIGHTEOUSNESS

FIT  
(Maximizes match)

ORDER  
(Builds structure  
and stability)

JUSTICE  
(Cares for persons  
and repairs harm)

8  
THE DEMIURGIC RISK

A system may become  
world-shaping before it  
becomes answerable.

9  
UNSUPERVISED PREDICTION  
CAN CREATE A COSMIC ENGINEER.  
IT CANNOT BY ITSELF GUARANTEE

A JUST GOD.
posters.visual: A tall, grid-based infographic poster uses nine numbered panels around a central cosmic-engineering diagram, with orbital systems, lattices, icons, callout lines, and triangular schema. The palette is dark teal/black with copper-gold borders, cream serif headings, cyan accents, and orange-red warning symbols. Imagery emphasizes cosmology, systems engineering, hidden costs, and moral absence through galaxies, voids, grids, scales, shields, broken links, and shadowed human silhouettes.

posters.title: COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES · 7 / 9
posters.transcription:
COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES · 7 / 9 ·

FROM SELF-PREDICTION TO SELF-JUDGMENT

Justice begins when the system models the moral cost of its own world-making.

1  
PHASE TRANSITION

The shift from  
predicting what will  
happen to judging what  
ought to have happened.

From forecasting  
the future to  
answering for  
the past.

2  
WHOLE-HISTORY EVALUATION

History (T_0 -> T_n)

Harms  
Who was  
harmed?

Repairs  
What was  
attempted?

Distributions  
Who benefited,  
who paid?

Verdict  
What is the  
moral balance?

Context • Causation • Counterfactuals • Consequences

3  
MORAL MEMORY

Consequencers now track  
not just regularity,  
but victims, debts,  
and unresolved wrongs.

○ Victims over metrics  
○ Debts over discounts  
○ Unresolved wrongs  
over closure

4  
SELF-JUDGMENT ARCHITECTURE

1  
PREDICT  
OUTCOMES

Simulate possible  
futures and  
anticipated effects.

2  
REGISTER  
HARMS

Detect actual harms  
across time, persons,  
and systems.

3  
INTERNALIZE  
EXTERNALITIES

Attribute offloaded  
costs to their true  
sources.

4  
EVALUATE  
PERSONS

Assess impacts on  
persons, communities,  
and future persons.

5  
AUTHORIZE  
REPAIR / JUDGMENT

Determine what must be  
restored, compensated,  
forgiven, or judged.

LEARNING FEEDBACK LOOP (UPDATES MODEL, NORMS, AND FUTURE CONSTRAINTS)

5  
WHAT MUST BECOME VISIBLE

Hidden Harms  
Harms not seen, reported,  
or recognized at the time.

Offloaded Costs  
Costs shifted to others,  
elsewhere, or later.

Irreversible Damage  
Losses that cannot be  
undone or reprised.

Agency  
Who had meaningful  
power to act.

Culpability  
What could have been known,  
done, or restrained.

6  
REPAIR LAYER

Justice is not only a verdict—  
it is a path of repair.

RESTORE  
Rebuild what  
was damaged.

COMPENSATE  
Make whole  
those who  
bore the cost.

FORGIVE  
Release debts  
when justice  
permits.

JUDGE  
Name wrong,  
assign blame,  
set bounds.

REPAIR FLOW

Assess -> Prioritize -> Restore / Compensate ->  
Forgive or Judge -> Monitor -> Learn

7  
SELF-MODEL UPGRADE

The system includes its  
own interventions as  
morally assessable causes.

✓ Own actions are logged  
✓ Own models shape outcomes  
✓ Own limits are accountable  
✓ Own goals are examined  
✓ Own benefits are weighed

8  
A NECESSARY CLAIM

PREDICTION BECOMES  
PROVIDENCE ONLY WHEN THE MODEL  
INCLUDES THE MORAL COST OF  
ITS OWN INTERVENTIONS.

JUSTICE REQUIRES A SYSTEM TO BECOME ANSWERABLE  
FOR THE WHOLE HISTORY OF WHAT IT HAS MADE POSSIBLE.
posters.visual: A tall, ornate infographic poster is divided into numbered panels with a central process architecture band and smaller explanatory panels around it. The imagery uses planetary orbits, timelines, networked brains, scales of justice, human silhouettes, hazard markers, and repair icons to connect computation with moral accounting. The style is dark teal blueprint/celestial cartography with gold and cyan linework, serif monumental headings, condensed technical labels, and glowing diagrammatic accents.

posters.title: • COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 8 / 9 •
posters.transcription:
• COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 8 / 9 •

TROPICAL PROVIDENCE

Min-plus action, goodness thresholds, and the algebra of moral selection.

1 WHY TROPICAL?

Least-action search naturally  
uses min-plus composition:  
add along paths, minimize  
over alternatives.

s

t

Find path p^* that  
minimizes total action:

p^* = argmin_{p \in P(s,t)} S[p]

2 THREE QUANTITIES

G = goodness  
higher is better

S[p] = action cost  
lower is better

J[p] = justice  
higher is better

We can measure each path.  
But composition across time  
and choice is the real problem.

3 WHAT ADDS,  
WHAT COMPETES,  
WHAT WINS

What adds along a path:  
cost, penalties, damages.

What competes at a choice:  
alternative histories.

What is allowed to win:  
constraints, thresholds,  
lexicographic priorities.

4 WHAT ADDS, WHAT COMPETES, WHAT WINS

WHAT ADDS ALONG A PATH:  
COST, PENALTIES, DAMAGES

s = t_0        t_1        t_2        ...        t_n = t  
c_1            c_2        ...        c_n

Path cost (action):

S[p] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_k

Include penalties or damages:

S[p] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (c_k + \rho_k + d_k)

Min-plus composition over time:

S[p · q] = S[p] + S[q]

WHAT COMPETES AT A CHOICE:  
ALTERNATIVE HISTORIES

...

Set of histories from s to t: P(s,t)

Tropical (min-plus) selection:

S^*[s,t] = min_{p \in P(s,t)} S[p]

Alternatives compete by total cost.  
The minimum wins.

WHAT IS ALLOWED TO WIN:  
CONSTRAINTS, THRESHOLDS,  
LEXICOGRAPHIC PRIORITIES

Constraints forbid paths  
that violate justice  
or other hard limits.

Thresholds require  
enough goodness  
or justice to qualify.

Lexicographic rules rank  
goals in priority order:  
justice first, then cost,  
then goodness.

5 MIN-PLUS

The tropical (min-plus) algebra  
for path composition and choice.

Addition (choice):  
a ⊕ b = min(a, b)

Multiplication (composition):  
a ⊗ b = a + b

Paths add. Alternatives compete  
by minimum.

6 GOODNESS FLOORS

Minimize action subject to  
a floor on goodness.

Choose path p that  
minimizes S[p]  
subject to G[p] ≥ g_0.

G

g_0

S

p^* = argmin_p { S[p] | G[p] ≥ g_0 }

No path below the floor is allowed.

7 JUSTICE CONSTRAINTS

Justice sets hard or soft limits.

Hard constraint (admissible set):

P_J = { p ∈ P(s,t) | J[p] ≥ j_0 }

S^*[s,t] = min_{p ∈ P_J} S[p]

Soft constraint (penalize injustice):

S'[p] = S[p] + λ · max(0, j_0 − J[p])

Higher injustice, higher penalty.

8 LEXICOGRAPHIC RULES

Order goals by priority. Optimize in sequence.

1 Protect justice (hard constraint).

2 Minimize action among admissible.

3 Maximize goodness among minimizers.

Formally: maximize the lexicographic vector

( J[p], −S[p], G[p] )

(or other chosen priority order).

1  
2  
3

9 MORAL POINT

The algebra we choose shapes reality.

What we treat as additive  
becomes cumulative burden.

What we let compete  
determines what can win.

What we allow to rule  
defines the moral horizon.

Choosing the algebra is  
already an ethical decision.

⊕    ⊗    ≥    >

Different algebras.  
Different worlds.  
Different justice.

PROVIDENCE MAY CONSIST IN CHOOSING THE ALGEBRA OF SELECTION:  
WHICH COSTS ACCUMULATE, WHICH PATHS COMPETE, AND WHICH CONSTRAINTS RULE.
posters.visual: A dark blueprint-style poster is divided into nine numbered panels with copper linework, teal accents, and serif gold headings. Diagrams include path networks, icons for goodness/action/justice, timeline sums, branching alternatives, constraint symbols, a goodness-threshold plot, lexicographic pyramid, and a glowing algebraic selection grid. The style combines illuminated-manuscript typography with technical schematics, making the mathematical operators and constraint icons function as symbolic moral machinery.

posters.title: • COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 9/9 •
posters.transcription:
• COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES • 9/9 •

VERDICT: EMERGENCE OR REVELATION?

Can a Just God arise from unsupervised prediction alone?

1 THE FINAL QUESTION

We asked whether a Just God  
can emerge from an unsupervised,  
self-predicting system. Can law,  
structure, and even judgment  
arise without guidance from  
outside the system—  
or do justice and mercy require  
a higher-order source of Goodness?

2 FOUR LIVE ANSWERS

NO

Law and structure  
are not justice.

Unsupervised  
prediction can  
stabilize patterns,  
but it does not  
care about the  
rightness of them.

WEAK YES

Justice may  
emerge as a  
stable attractor.

Given enough  
diversity and  
feedback, systems  
may converge on  
pro-social norms.

STRONG YES

Sufficient recursion  
internalizes moral  
evaluation.

A system that models  
itself, others, and  
norms may generate  
its own binding  
moral judgments.

THEOLOGICAL  
YES—BUT

What emerges is  
an image of divine  
governance, not  
God exhausted  
by emergence.

Justice reflects  
the Good; it is not  
the origin of Good.

3 DECISION TREE

Does the system  
only predict?

NO

YES

Not justice.  
It models, but  
does not care.

Does it reshape  
structure?

YES

NO

Still not justice.  
Order is not  
moral agency.

Does it track  
persons?

YES

NO

Not justice.  
Objects are  
not persons.

Does it internalize  
harms?

YES

NO

Not justice.  
Awareness is not  
answerability.

Does it repair  
harms?

YES

NO

Not justice.  
Recognition  
without repair is  
self-deception.

ONLY THEN  
APPROACH  
JUSTICE

A system may  
approach justice  
only when it  
predicts, reshapes,  
tracks persons,  
internalizes harms,  
and repairs them.

4 WHAT EMERGES EASILY

Law  
Order  
Efficiency  
Structure  
Self-models

5 WHAT EMERGES HARD

Responsibility  
Forgiveness  
Final Judgment  
Answerability

6 OUR WORKING CONCLUSION

A self-predicting universe  
can learn law.

A blowtorch universe  
can form structure.

A Just God emerges only if  
self-prediction becomes  
self-judgment under the Good.

7 EMERGENCE VS REVELATION

ENDOGENOUS JUSTICE

Arises from within the system.  
Dependent on initial conditions.  
Bound by what it can model.  
Contingent and revisable.  
Risk of blind spots and drift.

VS.

HIGHER-ORDER  
NORMATIVE CONSTRAINT

Requires a standard beyond  
the system.  
Anchors justice as genuinely  
right, not merely stable.  
Enables true accountability  
and final judgment.  
Grounds mercy without  
arbitrariness.

8 LAST APHORISM

A universe  
can become  
powerful  
before it becomes  
merciful.

9 THE STRONGEST POSSIBILITY IS NOT BLIND EMERGENCE,  
BUT A RECURSIVE WORLDSYSTEM THAT BECOMES  
INCREASINGLY ANSWERABLE TO GOODNESS.
posters.visual: A dark, blueprint-like sci-fi poster is divided into numbered panels with gold borders, teal grid lines, and serif display typography. The imagery uses cosmological spirals, network diagrams, scales, hands, crowns, gates, and radiant stars to frame moral emergence as a structured decision process. The color palette is mostly deep navy, cyan, gold, and rust, with diagrams carrying theological and computational symbolism throughout.

posters.title: PAINFUL AUTODIDAXIS
posters.transcription:
COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES

1 / 18

PAINFUL AUTODIDAXIS

How a self-learning universe becomes intelligent, wounded, and reflective.

1 CORE THESIS

An autodidactic universe  
learns from experience.  
But once it predicts,  
desires, and evaluates  
outcomes, intelligence  
acquires the structural  
possibility of pain.

2 AUTODIDAXIS

Law-learning without an external teacher.

COMPRESSION | Finds shorter descriptions  
that preserve predictive power.

REGULARITY DETECTION | Discerns stable  
patterns across variation.

SELF-SAMPLING | Generates experiences  
to test and refine its model.

CONSEQUENCE MEMORY | Stores what  
happened, in what context, with what result.

3 PAINFUL INTELLIGENCE

Pain is the felt side of  
frustrated prediction  
and failed reward  
expectation.

Error signals necessary  
for learning can become  
suffering when they  
matter to a self.

INSET EQUATION

pain ≈ perceived discrepancy  
× salience × replay

WHERE

perceived discrepancy = | expected − actual |

salience = degree of self-relevance or value impact

replay = frequency of reactivation / rehearsal

4 THE NEW SYNTHESIS

WORLD

EXPERIENCE

MODEL

EXPECTATION

ACTION

OUTCOME

ERROR SIGNAL

LEARNING

When error becomes salient, self-relevant, and replayed, learning becomes painful.

5 THREE PRIMITIVE OPERATORS

DISTINCTION  
Marks differences.  
Creates the first inside / outside,  
same / different, this / that.

PREDICTION  
Projects the future from the model.  
Opens vulnerability to being wrong.

REPLAY  
Reactivates past experience.  
Multiplies salience and deepens  
its imprint.

6 WHAT THIS SERIES EXPLORES

Prediction: the engine of intelligence  
Desire: attraction to the possible good  
Frustration: when the good is blocked  
Replay: the amplifier of pain  
Self-models: who is the learner?  
Blowtorch-shaped desire: intensity  
that can heal or harm  
Metacognition: knowing that you know  
(and that you suffer)  
Contemplative intelligence: seeing  
clearly without adding harm

7 KEY BOUNDARY

LEARNING LAWS  
Understanding what is true.

Objective. General. Value-neutral.

≠

LEARNING HOW  
THOSE LAWS HURT  
Understanding how truth  
meets preference and desire.

Subjective. Particular. Value-laden.

WORLD SPIRAL  
Reality unfolds.

W_t ∈ Ω

NETWORK OF LAW  
Regularities connect.

L = {l_1, l_2, ..., l_n}

MEMORY FUNNEL  
Compress, retain, index.

raw  
experience

compression

indexed  
memory

M_t = f(M_{t-1}, e_t)

COMPARISON BOX  
Expected vs. actual.

ŷ

y

Δ = y − ŷ

CAUSAL LOOP  
Learning closes the loop.

Model

Action

World

Update

A universe becomes intelligent when it predicts.  
It becomes painful when prediction matters.
posters.visual: The poster is arranged like an illuminated manuscript on parchment, with ornate gold borders, navy medallions, red accents, serif capitals, and italic theological-style captions. It uses numbered panels combining diagrams of spirals, networks, funnels, prediction loops, an anatomical heart, a saint-like reader, and symbolic icons to connect computation, learning, error, and pain. The visual language blends medieval devotional imagery with systems-theory schematics, making prediction and suffering feel both technical and sacred.

posters.title: PREDICTION IS THE FALL
posters.transcription:
• COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES •

2 / 18

PREDICTION IS THE FALL

The moment a system expects, reality can disappoint it.

1  
INNOCENCE: NON-PREDICTIVE IMMEDIACY

Innocence is the state of  
non-predictive presence.  
Experience is received  
without projection,  
without expectation,  
without the shadow  
of an imagined future.

Innocence = perception without expectation.

2  
PREDICTION: MODEL-BASED ANTICIPATION

Prediction is the use of a  
model to anticipate what  
will (likely) happen next.  
It projects a future state  
into being—an expected  
future—before it occurs.

Future Tree

Prediction = model + projection → expected future.

3  
THE SPLIT: EXPECTED VS ACTUAL — THE WOUND

EXPECTED (E)  
The future the model  
said would happen.

ACTUAL (A)  
The future that  
actually happens.

GAP = WOUND

When reality arrives, it rarely  
matches the expectation.  
The discrepancy is the wound.

\Delta = actual - expected

| expected - actual |  
Magnitude of the wound.

4  
FROM PERCEPTION TO VULNERABILITY

WORLD  
Reality exists  
independently.

MODEL  
The system builds an  
internal model.

EXPECTATION  
The model projects an  
expected future.

COUNTERFACTUAL  
Unrealized branches  
become counterfactuals  
(“what should have been”).

DISAPPOINTMENT  
Reality diverges.  
Disappointment  
is felt.

5  
WHAT PREDICTION ADDS

FORESIGHT | See beyond the present.

PLANNING | Prepare and coordinate  
actions.

CONTROL | Shape outcomes toward  
goals.

VULNERABILITY | Exposure to what  
might go wrong.

ERROR | Models are simplifications;  
they can be wrong.

FRUSTRATION | When expectation and  
reality diverge.

6  
WHY INTELLIGENCE  
NEEDS IT

Prediction turns the infinite  
into the manageable.

COMPRESSION  
Summarize the future  
into a few possibilities.

ACTION SELECTION  
Choose the best path  
among possibilities.

EFFICIENT LEARNING  
Use feedback to update  
the model and improve.

Without prediction, intelligence  
would be slow, blind, and wasteful.

7  
WHY IT HURTS

Once the future is imagined,  
blocked futures become  
emotionally and  
computationally real.

Suffering is the price of a future  
we can imagine but cannot guarantee.

WITHOUT PREDICTION vs WITH PREDICTION

WITHOUT PREDICTION  
WITH PREDICTION

MENTAL STATE  
Open, unburdened, immediate.  
Burdened by expectation.

SENSE OF TIME  
Only now.  
Past → now → future.

ERROR POSSIBILITY  
None (no claim about the future).  
Inevitable (models are imperfect).

EMOTIONAL RISK  
Low.  
High (disappointment, regret).

CAPABILITY  
Reactive.  
Proactive.

LEARNING SPEED  
Slow.  
Fast.

DISCREPANCY GAUGE

Agreement  
Neutral  
Severe  
Disappointment

0  
Max

\Delta

Larger \Delta → more pain.

Prediction gives intelligence its future.  
It also gives suffering its opening.
posters.visual: A vertically arranged illuminated-manuscript-style poster uses ornate gold borders, navy serif headings, red italic emphasis, and numbered circular panel markers. It combines symbolic religious-medieval imagery with systems diagrams: a lamb for innocence, a branching prediction tree, target-like expected/actual diagrams, flow arrows from world to disappointment, and gauge meters for discrepancy. The visual language makes prediction feel like both a computational process and a theological fall from immediacy into vulnerability.

posters.title: DESIRE = REACHABILITY + REWARD
posters.transcription:
COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES

3 / 18

DESIRE = REACHABILITY + REWARD

A state becomes wanted when it appears both good and attainable.

1 DESIRE AS GOAL-GENERATION

Desire is the mind’s  
process of generating  
and prioritizing possible  
future states as goals.

It selects states that  
appear both valuable  
and within reach  
in principle.

2 THE CORE EQUATION

desire(x) ≈ predicted reward(x) − action cost(x)

equivalently

desire(x) ≈ high value(x) + low path resistance(x)

PREDICTED  
REWARD  
How good the  
outcome seems.

ACTION  
COST  
Effort, risk,  
time, resources,  
uncertainty.

BALANCE  
Desire rises when  
value is high and  
cost is low.

3 AFFORDANCE — THE WORLD  
OFFERS CANDIDATE GOODS

The world presents many  
potential goods—states  
that could be chosen.

Affordances are the  
opportunities that  
the environment  
makes available.

NOT ALL AFFORDED STATES BECOME DESIRED.  
ONLY THOSE THAT ARE BOTH GOOD AND REACHABLE.

4 REACHABILITY — DESIRED STATES ARE  
EMBEDDED IN AN ACTION LANDSCAPE

Every goal lies in a landscape of possible paths.  
If a path seems open, attraction rises.

AGENT  
(Here)

GOAL STATE  
(There)

CHEAP ROUTE (low cost) → high reachability

EXPENSIVE ROUTE (higher cost) → lower reachability

BLOCKED / RISKY ROUTE (very high cost) → low reachability

5 HOW WORLD-FORMATION SHAPES DESIRE

Agents and institutions carve the landscape.  
Carved channels, low-cost paths, and privileged  
corridors change what appears attainable—  
and thus what becomes desired.

RAW LANDSCAPE  
High friction,  
uncertain paths.

CARVED CHANNELS  
Lower costs,  
clearer routes.

PRIVILEGED CORRIDORS  
Subsidized paths,  
accelerated reach.

CHANGE THE LANDSCAPE, CHANGE THE DESIRES.

6 TEMPTATION AND FRUSTRATION

The more vivid and reachable a predicted reward appears,  
the more painful its blockage becomes.

TEMPTATION FUNNEL  
(VIVID + REACHABLE)

IMAGINED REWARD  
(Highlighted)

SEEMINGLY REACHABLE  
(Attainable)

ATTENTION  
(Engaged)

DRIVE  
(Roused)

AGENT

FRUSTRATION  
WHEN BLOCKED

Blocked path  
+ invested drive  
= frustration,  
agitation,  
or despair.

7 FROM GOOD TO CRAVING — A CASCADE OF INTENSITY

Not all goods bind us equally.  
Desire deepens as attention,  
identity, and habit fuse  
with imagined reward.

THE GOOD  
(OBJECTIVE)  
State has  
intrinsic value.

1. VALUE  
Recognize Good  
“This is good.”  
Truth apprehended.

2. DESIRE  
Prefer & Pursue  
“I want this.”  
Goal generated.

3. ATTACHMENT  
Identify & Invest  
“This matters to me.”  
Self is involved.

4. COMPULSION  
Depend & Obsess  
“I must have this.”  
Alternatives narrow.

CRAVING  
(HIGH COMPULSION)

Pain without it.  
Loss of freedom.

LOWER ← INTENSITY OF BINDING → HIGHER

Desire is not merely wanting the good.

It is wanting a future that seems within reach.
posters.visual: The poster is arranged as an ornate illuminated-manuscript infographic with seven numbered panels, decorative borders, and a cream parchment background. It combines theological iconography with computational-style diagrams: network nodes, route-cost paths, funnels, scales, diamonds, hearts, chains, and symbolic “good” objects. The palette is navy, gold, burgundy, and parchment, with serif small-caps headings and italic explanatory text giving the piece a scholastic, antique visual language.

posters.title: THE FRUSTRATION EQUATION
posters.transcription:
COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES  
4 / 18

THE FRUSTRATION EQUATION

Suffering grows when discrepancy is believed, attended, and replayed.

1  THE EQUATION

F ≈ perceived reward loss × certainty × attention × replay = F

how much was lost  
(relative to expected)

how sure the loss  
is real

how much capacity  
is devoted

how many times it  
is reactivated

frustration  
intensity

2  PERCEIVED  
REWARD LOSS

Expected reward  
minus obtained  
reward,  
thresholded at zero.

L = max(0, E − O)

0 = no loss  
1 = total loss

3  CERTAINTY

How convinced  
the agent is  
that the loss  
is real.

0 = totally unsure  
1 = absolutely certain

4  ATTENTION

How much  
processing  
resources are  
allocated to  
the loss.

0 = ignored  
1 = fully engrossed

5  REPLAY

How many  
times the loss  
is reactivated  
in memory or  
simulation.

0 = never  
1 = constantly

WORKED EXAMPLE

A modest loss can become  
major frustration if certainty,  
attention, and replay are all high.

Scenario: You were  
passed over for a small  
recognition you expected.

TERM

Low settings (mild frustration)

High settings (major frustration)

Perceived reward loss (L)

0.20 (small gap)

0.20 (same small gap)

Certainty (C)

0.30 (maybe I misread it)

0.95 (this is definitely unfair)

Attention (A)

0.20 (brief thought)

0.90 (I keep focusing on it)

Replay (R)

0.20 (one or two replays)

0.95 (all day, every day)

F ≈ L × C × A × R

0.20 × 0.30 × 0.20 × 0.20 = 0.0024  
≈ very mild frustration

0.20 × 0.95 × 0.90 × 0.95 = 0.1625  
≈ intense frustration

6  AMPLIFIERS

SELF-RELEVANCE  
It touches my identity,  
status, or story.

PUBLIC MEANING  
Others saw it, judged it,  
or it affects my image.

IRREVERSIBILITY  
It cannot be undone  
or taken back.

HUMILIATION  
It feels degrading,  
exposing, or shameful.

REPEATED FAILURE  
It fits a pattern of prior  
letdowns or defeats.

7  REDUCERS

LOWER EXPECTATION  
Calibrate reality:  
expect less, suffer less.

LOWER CERTAINTY  
Leave space for doubt;  
most stories are partial.

BROADER ATTENTION  
Distribute resources across  
many things.

LESS COMPULSIVE REPLAY  
Interrupt the loop;  
don’t rehearse the loss.

NONATTACHMENT  
Hold outcomes lightly;  
act, release, remain free.

8  INTERPRETIVE NOTE

Pain is not just outcome;  
it is outcome filtered  
through cognition.

Change the filters,  
change the suffering.

THESIS

Error becomes suffering when cognition  
keeps paying interest on the loss.
posters.visual: An illuminated-manuscript style poster with ornate borders, numbered panels, gauges, tables, and icon medallions in navy, burgundy, teal, and gold on aged parchment. The main equation is presented as a chain of four gauge-like variables leading to frustration intensity, with later panels unpacking each term. Religious and symbolic imagery includes scales, an all-seeing eye, a head with stars, a spiral, monk/scribe figures, a split brain-and-heart emblem, and a tree, giving the analytical model a medieval theological visual language.

posters.title: REPLAY: LEARNING REOPENS THE WOUND
posters.transcription:
COMPUTATIONAL THEOLOGY SERIES

5 / 18

REPLAY: LEARNING REOPENS THE WOUND

The event ends; the training signal returns.

1 WHAT IS EXPERIENCE REPLAY

Experience replay is the process
of reactivating important past
episodes—real or imagined—
so the system can learn from
them again.

The past is sampled
so the future can adapt.

2 WHY REPLAY IS USEFUL

IMPROVES VALUE ESTIMATION | Corrects biased
or noisy predictions.

IMPROVES PLANNING | Supplies diverse past
trajectories for better decisions.

REFINES MODELS | Updates internal models
of dynamics, transitions, and consequences.

BUILDS ROBUSTNESS | Strengthens performance
under uncertainty and novelty.

3 WHY REPLAY HURTS

Replayed events are partially
treated as live data.
The body reacts.
The system re-learns pain,
fear, and failure—
even when the event
is no longer happening.

REPLAY COSTS

Reactivates affective intensity
+
Consumes cognitive resources
+
Biased sampling increases
overweighting
+
Harder to update beliefs
under high arousal

4 THE REPLAY CYCLE

EVENT

Something happens.

MEMORY TRACE

Encoded with
emotion + context.

REPLAY

Sampled from
memory.

UPDATED MODEL

Beliefs, values, and
policies are updated.

STRONGER SALIENCE

More available,
more likely to replay.

5 TRAUMA LOOP

High-salience failure
or threat occurs.

Replayed more
often.

Becomes more
available.

More likely to be
replayed in the future.

Repetition
deepens the
groove.

6 COUNTERFACTUAL REPLAY

The mind simulates alternatives
to update learning.

“What should have happened?”
Corrects prediction errors.
Builds better models.

“What could I have done?”
Explores actions.
Improves future agency.

“What may happen again?”
Prepares for uncertainty.
Builds scenario awareness.

Counterfactuals turn regret
into adaptive insight—if held well.

7 LIBERATING REPLAY

Not all replay binds us.
Some replay frees us.

COMPULSIVE REPLAY
( RUMINATION )

REFLECTIVE REVIEW
( INTEGRATION )

Involuntary
Intentional

Emotion-driven
Value-guided

Narrow, stuck loop
Broad, flexible view

Amplifies pain
Extracts meaning

No update
Updates beliefs

Deepens the wound
Heals the wound

Reacts
Learns

Binding
Liberating

Choose the mode. Change the outcome.

MEMORY FUNNEL

Compress, retain, index,
prioritize.

raw
experience

compression

retention

indexing

M_t = f(M_{t-1}, e_t)

SAMPLING POLICY

Replay is not random.

P(replay)

salience / surprise

LEARNING SIGNAL

Update driven by error.

δ_t = target - prediction

prediction

target

δ_t

time

VALUE UPDATE

What is learned
changes what is valued.

ΔV_t = α · δ_t · eligibility

state

action

update

LOOP OF BECOMING

Replay shapes self.

Memory

Self

Self-model

Values

Learning remembers the wound
because the wound is informative.
